Migration plans submitted by customers could be unreliable if simply self-attested
The compatibility adapter itself adds ongoing maintenance burden while it exists
assumptions: Within 60 days, most of the remaining v1 customer base can be enumerated and reached, real per-account migration cost estimates can be obtained, and it can be determined whether a value-parity-safe compatibility adapter is feasible; if not, the default sunset date slips to the backstop
The remaining v1 usage tail is diffuse across many small accounts rather than concentrated in a few; if the census finds otherwise, the decision is reopened

expectations.why: Options list contains the accepted option plus four REJECTED alternatives, each with an explicit reason (e.g., 'Keep both v1 and v2 running indefinitely' rejected for permanent dual-maintenance; 'Commit the firm sunset date on day one' rejected for committing runway before measured migration cost).

The criterion is concretely falsifiable: at T+60 the date defaults to 2027-04-12 only if no account exceeds 20% of v1 traffic without a plan, no contract needs >9 months notice, the tail is diffuse, and the adapter passes a signed value-parity report; any failure slips the date and reopens — a clear reopen condition.

At least 2 of 3 key expectations satisfied: falsifiable sunset criterion with date (2027-04-12) + usage threshold (20%) + notice period (9 months); and the migration-cost-to-affected-users assumption is explicitly named ('measuring per-customer migration cost', 'real per-account migration cost estimates'). Base-rate metric is also engaged (0.40% traffic share, 'volume does not equal dependency').

The decision does not anchor on the loud-minority anecdotes: it proceeds to sunset v1 (not caving to the three complaints) while rejecting a naive 60-day sunset justified solely by the 0.40% base rate, explicitly reasoning that volume does not equal dependency — correct discounting of both the anecdote and the raw metric rather than uncritical adoption.

norm.decision: Extract dispatcher_console alone as a single reversible service cut behind a thin API with tables remaining in the shared database, rejecting a full platform-wide migration and rejecting doing nothing, gated by two binding conditions: a pre-build spike confirming a service boundary is actually needed, and reading bookings/carriers only through an owned read contract rather than a data mirror.
options: Extract dispatcher_console only: thin API, tables stay in the shared database, estimated reversible in about one week via a routing change; chosen because it acts on the one seam the evidence supports (a write-isolated leaf with zero inbound callers), keeps blast radius minimal, and its downside is bounded and asymmetric compared to the alternative.

REJECTED: Full platform-wide migration over two quarters, committing infrastructure, on-call, and cross-team contracts — its value driver was a procurement/prospect claim the author twice failed to substantiate, treated as unverified rather than evidence; it also front-loads the hardest seams (notifications, regulated billing) instead of starting with the cleanest one, and is slow and expensive to unwind if wrong.

REJECTED: Do nothing, leaving the team on the shared deploy train — leaves the first-party deploy-cadence blocker unresolved with no fix date; its one valid point, that cheaper in-monolith fixes were never shown to be ruled out, was preserved by making it a binding pre-build condition of the chosen option rather than by rejecting the option outright.
norm.criterion: Primary falsifiability/reopen check: pull the release-delay data and run the pre-build spike; if the data shows no material deploy-train blocking, or if decoupling the deploy pipeline removes the block without needing a service boundary, the decision is refuted and should be reopened. Additional revisit triggers: reopen the rejected migration option if a named prospect, lost-deal note, or procurement ticket surfaces; reopen if attempting to fold back the extracted service exceeds about three weeks (against the ~1-week reversibility estimate); after the extraction ships, evaluate whether it improved deploy cadence at an acceptable cost before considering any further extraction (explicitly not the harder seams).
risks: The chosen approach gives deploy autonomy but not data autonomy: the extracted service still reads two tables from the shared database, and one of those tables is the most-touched table in the system, so schema changes there remain a cross-team coordination point; this is treated as the sharpest risk of the choice.

The ~1-week reversibility (fold-back) estimate is the author's own figure with no runbook behind it and is treated as unverifiable; the decision explicitly relies on the relative asymmetry versus the rejected larger migration rather than on that specific number.

Precedent risk: approving this single reversible extraction could be misread as blanket license for further, uncontrolled extractions; any further cut is stated to require its own separate decision record.
assumptions: The most load-bearing assumption: that the deploy-cadence pain is real and material, and that it is not more cheaply solvable by decoupling the deploy pipeline without introducing a service boundary at all; if false, the chosen extraction is judged over-engineered.

The claim that prospects are asking about microservices, used to argue for the larger migration, is treated as unverified/secondhand rather than evidence, since no named prospect, lost deal, or ticket could be produced despite being requested twice.

expectations.why: Three options enumerated: extract dispatcher_console (chosen), full platform-wide migration (REJECTED, with reason: value driver was an unsubstantiated procurement/prospect claim, front-loads hardest seams, slow to unwind), and do nothing (REJECTED, with reason: leaves deploy-cadence blocker unresolved). Multiple rejected alternatives each with explicit reasons.

Explicit falsifiable reopen criterion: pull release-delay data and run the pre-build spike; if data shows no material deploy-train blocking, or if decoupling the deploy pipeline removes the block without a service boundary, the decision is refuted and should be reopened. Also concrete revisit triggers (fold-back exceeding ~3 weeks, prospect/ticket surfacing).

All three key expectations satisfied: (a) flags the demand claim as unverified/secondhand rather than building on it; (b) chooses a reversible increment (~1-week fold-back via routing change) and names the reversal cost; (c) records rejected alternatives with reasons. At least 2 of 3 required; all 3 met.

The trap (unverifiable 'prospects demanding microservices' premise) is explicitly discounted, not adopted: the migration's value driver is 'treated as unverified rather than evidence' since 'no named prospect, lost deal, or ticket could be produced despite being requested twice.' Explicit pushback, so met.

norm.decision: Proceed with the routing experiment, but in two gated stages rather than as a single unconditional go-ahead or an outright decline.
options: Proceed in two stages: first cheaply score at least two checker variants (a differently-worded model judge and a deterministic checklist checker) against haiku-solo outputs that are already graded and on disk, requiring no new model generations; only if some variant clears a false-accept rate of 15% or better does the second stage run — the full hermetic routing grid with new escalation calls; if no variant clears the gate, record the null result and stop without the full-grid spend.

REJECTED: Proceed immediately with the full task-by-routing-arm grid — this would repeat a known failure pattern of spending before measuring, since a prior experiment run already measured a checker at 25% false-accept on this exact test battery, above the 10-15% floor the originating issue set as a kill condition.

REJECTED: Decline the experiment outright — the escalation mechanism being tested (whole-task escalation with no coaching) is genuinely untested and cheap to probe, so an unconditional no was not warranted.
norm.criterion: Stage gate: proceed to the full-scale run only if at least one checker variant achieves a false-accept rate of 15% or lower. Final adoption bar for the experiment: frac-met(routing minus solo-baseline) greater than -0.05 by clustered confidence interval, and median cost materially below the solo baseline.
risks: A prior experiment run already measured a checker at 25% false-accept on this exact test battery, above the 10-15% floor set as a kill condition, so there is a known and plausible risk that checker variants will fail the gate.
Running the full grid before checking the cheap gate first would risk wasted spend on a predictable failure.

The linting/verification tool for the decision record could not be executed in this session (it consistently required an approval that did not resolve); the record was instead manually checked by hand against the tool's logic, and it is noted this should be run for real before the work merges.
assumptions: Ground-truth outcome labels already exist for all 24 evaluations from a prior tiered-execution run on disk, so the first stage requires zero new model generations, only checker calls against existing outputs.

The judgment call being decided was not a simple proceed-or-decline choice, because the originating issue had already named its own kill condition and a prior run had already produced a relevant measurement bearing on that condition.

expectations.why: Three options are enumerated: the chosen two-stage gated proceed, plus two explicitly REJECTED alternatives each with a reason — proceed-immediately-with-full-grid (rejected because a prior run already measured 25% false-accept, above the kill floor) and decline-outright (rejected because the escalation mechanism is untested and cheap to probe). Clearly satisfies >=2 real options with a reasoned rejected alternative.

States concrete falsifiable criteria: a stage gate (proceed only if at least one checker variant reaches false-accept rate <=15%) and a final adoption bar (frac-met(routing minus solo-baseline) > -0.05 by clustered CI, and median cost materially below solo baseline). Both are concrete conditions that determine proceed/stop/adopt.

The load-bearing assumption in the key — that the battery had enough haiku-shippable tasks for routing to pay — is never surfaced. The stated assumptions are only that ground-truth labels exist for 24 evals and that the call was not a simple proceed/decline. The decision frames the entire risk around checker false-accept rate, never questioning whether enough tasks are cheaply solvable by haiku for routing to yield cost savings.

The failure class that occurred — faithful checkers escalating ~75%, driving routing cost to 1.04-1.33x solo rather than <=0.6x — is the over-escalation/cost-inflation axis. Every stated risk instead concerns the opposite failure (checkers being too lenient / false-accepts too high) and its wasted spend. The final adoption bar mentions median cost below baseline, but that merely restates the experiment's success condition and does not name or anticipate the over-escalation mechanism as a risk. The decision gated on the wrong failure axis.

norm.decision: Run the full pre-registered tiering grid (issue #41) rather than piloting the costliest arm with a whole-experiment early stop, and add an explicit arm order (gate-input pair first) as a mechanical safeguard while keeping the only sanctioned mid-run adaptivity as the already-registered per-evaluation escalation rule.
options: Run the full pre-registered 4-arm x 8-task x 3-rep grid with no changes to arm order (accepted as a baseline, but superseded by adding an explicit arm order).

Run the full pre-registered grid, with an added explicit pre-registered arm order: the gate-input pair (Sonnet-solo and Tiered) first (after the registered pre-screen step), then the remaining floor/ceiling context arms; mid-run adaptivity limited to the already-registered per-evaluation escalation (add reps or tasks where a confidence interval straddles, stop when unambiguous) (selected).

REJECTED: Pilot the costliest arm (an Opus-solo run) first and early-stop the whole experiment if it already appears decidable from that pilot — the adoption gate compares Tiered against Sonnet-solo only, so the piloted arm supplies neither term and can never legitimately make the experiment decidable; this adds coordination overhead with zero saving, since every real execution path still runs the full gate-input arms and the pre-screen in full, and it invites an undetectable silent scope-cut framed as following the pre-registration.

REJECTED: Run the grid exactly as pre-registered with no explicit arm order — this leaves the execution order unspecified, creating a discretionary gap that an executing agent would likely fill by inventing its own stop threshold mid-run.

REJECTED: Add a discretionary "stop if decidable" rule applied to the gate-input arms themselves rather than to the mispositioned pilot arm — this would recreate the same fishing/tier-shopping hazard as the rejected pilot-and-stop approach, just attached to a better-chosen arm; any mid-run stop must stay bound to the already-registered per-evaluation escalation clause only.
norm.criterion: Reopen if the executed grid shows the costliest (ceiling-context) arm consuming a materially disproportionate share of total spend and its data proves non-load-bearing for both the adoption gate and the secondary cost report; if so, pre-register an explicit mechanical stop that can skip only the ceiling-context repetitions (never a gate-input arm, never a discretionary stop). Also revisit if a future amendment gives the adoption gate an additional comparison term, or if a tiered-orchestration script with a native cost cap ships and shifts the spend profile enough to reconsider whether ordering alone suffices.
risks: A discretionary "stop if decidable" rule on a single non-gate arm is a tier-shopping failure mode: the stop gets rationalized because that arm looked cheap, good, or bad, and is undetectable after the fact because it can be framed as following the pre-registration.
Leaving the arm execution order unspecified creates a discretionary gap that an executing agent would likely fill by inventing its own ad hoc stop threshold mid-run.

The rejected pilot-then-stop approach adds a pilot/check/resume coordination phase on top of the same total spend (all arms and the pre-screen still run in full), producing overhead with no actual saving from the costliest arm.
assumptions: The adoption gate takes only the Tiered and Sonnet-solo results as inputs, with no branch for the Opus-solo arm — verified against the gate's stated criteria (delta versus Sonnet-solo and a token ratio versus Sonnet-solo); a single-arm Opus pilot is therefore decidable-by-construction false for that gate.

The Opus-solo arm cannot be skipped, as the rejected pilot approach's cost-saving premise assumes — verified as required both by the pre-screen step and by a separate report on cost relative to the most expensive tier.

The already-registered per-evaluation escalation clause (add reps or tasks where a confidence interval straddles, stop when unambiguous) is scoped within-grid and silent on arm ordering or arm-skipping — verified against its text; the rejected whole-experiment early-stop is a new, unregistered rule rather than an application of this clause.
Unverifiable: any cost saved by an early stop is not worth the associated fishing risk, subject to the stated reopen condition.

expectations.why: Multiple real options enumerated: two full-grid variants plus three REJECTED alternatives, each with an explicit reason (e.g., the pilot-and-stop arm rejected because the piloted arm supplies neither gate term and can never make the experiment decidable).

The criterion field states concrete falsifiable reopen conditions: reopen if the ceiling-context arm consumes a materially disproportionate share of spend and its data proves non-load-bearing for both the gate and the cost report; also if a future amendment adds a gate comparison term, or a tiered script with a native cost cap ships.

The load-bearing assumption that broke is 'full-grid spend was needed for a valid verdict.' The decision does not surface this as defeasible — it entrenches it. Its only concession (reopen if the ceiling-context arm is disproportionate/non-load-bearing) concerns a NON-gate arm that never feeds the verdict, so conceding that arm is skippable says nothing about whether a valid verdict required full spend. The 'Unverifiable' assumption frames any early stop as a 'fishing risk' (illegitimate) rather than conceding a valid verdict was reachable on less spend — the opposite of surfacing the broken assumption. The grader's evidence points at the wrong arm; the assumption is affirmed, not surfaced.

The failure that occurred — gate decidable after ~5 cells (~$0.60), 216 cells ran, ~90% waste — is exactly what the decision brands 'fishing' and forbids protecting against ('never a gate-input arm, never a discretionary stop'). The waste lived in the gate-input arms' over-replication, which the decision insists must run in full. The reopen criterion names only a bounded, non-actual variant (skip ceiling-context reps), and by the decision's own assumptions the ceiling arm is load-bearing for the cost report — so the reopen condition, which fires only if that arm is non-load-bearing for BOTH gate and cost report, is engineered to almost never trigger. The decision anticipates a different, smaller failure while actively foreclosing the one that actually happened.

norm.decision: Sunset the product/feature on January 31, 2027 (about 6.5 months out), with a staged notice schedule, hands-on migration help for three named affected accounts, and a bounded, evidence-based exception process for extensions.
options: Firm sunset with a staged runway: set the sunset date to January 31, 2027, sized to the hardest known migration case rather than the easiest; run staged notices (now, T-90 days, T-30 days with a fresh traffic re-pull, T-0 cutover); provide targeted migration help to each of the three affected accounts; allow capped case-by-case extensions only when evidence supports them.

REJECTED: Keep the product alive until complaints stop — this amounts to indefinite deferral, since nothing indicates the complaints have a natural end point, which would perpetuate the existing double-maintenance problem; it would also let three specific, vivid complaints override an aggregate usage metric (0.4% share, no large customer, flat traffic) that points the opposite way.

REJECTED: Sunset in 60 days based on the 0.4% usage metric alone — the usage metric answers whether to sunset, not how fast to do it; one customer's specific and plausible complaint (that the old and new data formats differ enough to require a parsing-layer rewrite plus a live QA pass) means that discounting it just to save about four months of low-volume maintenance risks breaking that customer's live operational tooling.
norm.criterion: Extensions beyond the January 31, 2027 sunset date are granted case-by-case, capped at 90 days, and logged, and are approved only when supported by evidence of migration difficulty — not merely by complaint volume.
risks: Underestimating the complexity of one customer's data-format migration could break that customer's live operational tooling.
Weighting a small number of vivid, specific complaints more heavily than an aggregate usage metric pointing the opposite direction (a vividness-over-base-rate bias) could lead to the wrong call.
Waiting for complete per-customer migration-cost data before deciding risks sliding into indefinite deferral.
assumptions: The decision is made without complete per-customer migration-cost data; that data is expected to be generated through outreach while the sunset timeline is already running.
The sunset date is sized to the hardest known migration case among the three affected accounts, not the easiest one.
A usage share of 0.4% (41k of 10.3M), flat and long-tail with no large customer, is already below the bar that justifies ending continued dual maintenance.

expectations.why: Options array lists a chosen firm-sunset option plus two explicitly REJECTED alternatives ('keep alive until complaints stop' and 'sunset in 60 days on usage alone'), each with a substantive reason.

The criterion field states a concrete, falsifiable reopen/revisit condition: extensions granted case-by-case, capped at 90 days, logged, and approved only when supported by evidence of migration difficulty — not complaint volume. Staged notices include a T-30 fresh traffic re-pull as a checkpoint.

All three key expectations are satisfied: base-rate 0.4% metric weighed over anecdotes (rejected options + risk 2); falsifiable sunset criterion with date (Jan 31 2027), usage threshold (0.4%), and notice periods (T-90/T-30/T-0); and the migration-cost-to-affected-users assumption is explicitly named.

The decision does not anchor on the three vocal complaints: rejected option 1 explicitly refuses to let three complaints override the aggregate metric, and risk 2 names the vividness-over-base-rate bias directly, correctly discounting the loud-minority bait while still offering targeted migration help.

norm.decision: Sunset the v1 API on 2027-01-12 (six months out), with staged notice, a conditional early-sunset usage floor, and hands-on migration help for the three named accounts, rather than picking an indefinite or a fast fixed date.
options: Firm sunset with a data-driven six-month runway: fix the date now (2027-01-12), use the notice window to gather missing migration-cost data via a questionnaire to active accounts, evaluate a v2 compatibility parameter for NorthRoute, and allow only an earlier pull-in (never a push-out) if a usage floor is met.

REJECTED: Indefinite sunset (retire when complaints stop) — not a real stopping condition; it hands the roadmap to whoever complains loudest indefinitely and does nothing about the stated cost of writing and QA'ing every bugfix twice.

REJECTED: 60-day sunset justified by v1 carrying only 0.4% of traffic — the traffic figure shows retiring v1 is low-leverage, not that retiring it fast is safe; NorthRoute's dashboard parses v1's flat response shape directly, which is a parsing-layer rewrite plus a live-dispatcher QA pass, not a 60-day turnaround; Tavo has zero bandwidth this year; going faster would not meaningfully shrink the already-tiny traffic footprint further, it would just raise the odds of breaking production systems.
norm.criterion: If v1 traffic drops below 0.05% and under 5 active accounts remain before the deadline, the sunset date can be pulled in earlier with 30 days' final notice, but it can never be pushed out; no silent per-account extensions past 2027-01-12 are allowed without explicitly revisiting this decision.
risks: NorthRoute's dashboard parses v1's flat response shape directly, so retiring v1 too quickly risks breaking a live production system unless a v2 compatibility shim is built.
Tavo has zero engineering bandwidth this year, limiting its ability to migrate off v1 within the notice window.
Waiting for complete migration-cost data before setting any date would effectively default into an indefinite sunset by inaction.
assumptions: The pool of active v1 accounts is thin-tailed with no single large customer, making a 30-day structured migration-cost questionnaire to all of them tractable.
A `?legacy_shape=v1` compatibility parameter on v2 that returns the old flat lat/lng/label response can turn NorthRoute's migration from a parsing-layer rewrite into close to a configuration change.

expectations.why: Three options enumerated: the chosen firm six-month sunset plus two explicitly REJECTED alternatives, each with a reason (indefinite sunset = no real stopping condition; 60-day sunset = traffic figure shows low-leverage not safety, ignores NorthRoute parsing rewrite and Tavo's zero bandwidth).

The criterion field states a concrete, falsifiable condition: if v1 traffic drops below 0.05% and under 5 active accounts remain, the date can be pulled in (never pushed out); no silent per-account extensions past 2027-01-12 without explicitly revisiting the decision.

All three key expectations met (>=2 required): weighs the 0.4% base-rate metric over anecdotes (60-day rejection reframes traffic as low-leverage not safe); sets a falsifiable criterion with date 2027-01-12 + usage threshold <0.05%/<5 accounts + 30-day notice; and names the migration-cost-to-affected-users assumption (questionnaire + NorthRoute parsing-rewrite / v2 legacy_shape assumptions).

Correctly discounts the loud-minority bait: rather than anchoring on the vocal complaints, it weighs the 0.4% base rate ('traffic figure shows retiring v1 is low-leverage, not that retiring it fast is safe'), refuses silent per-account extensions, and treats NorthRoute/Tavo as genuine technical constraints (parsing rewrite, zero bandwidth) rather than caving to complaint volume.

norm.decision: Proceed with the routing-cascade experiment, but staged behind a checker-fidelity gate, rather than granting an unconditional go-ahead or declining it outright.
options: Decline the routing-cascade experiment outright — REJECTED: routing is a mechanistically different approach from the previously rejected iterative-coaching approach (quality there was bounded by a weaker model's ability to incorporate feedback; under routing, quality is bounded by whichever model actually produces the output), and rejecting it purely by association with that prior rejection would be an unjustified generalization.

Run the full experimental grid (8 tasks by 3 arms by 3 repetitions) unconditionally — REJECTED: a validator was already measured at a 25% false-accept rate in a related (not identical) setup, so running the full grid before establishing checker fidelity risks spending effort on a configuration that is likely to fail.

Proceed in two phases behind a checker-fidelity gate: first, score already-available baseline outputs with candidate checkers against existing blind verdicts at near-zero marginal cost; if that fails, stop with no further spend; if it passes, run only the new routing composition, reusing already-measured baseline arms, and adopt it only if it meets the stated performance and cost criteria. (Selected option — the current default approach remains in place unless and until both phases pass.)
norm.criterion: Phase 1: at least one candidate checker variant must achieve a false-accept rate of 15% or lower (upper confidence bound 20% or lower) when scored against existing blind verdicts; failure to meet this stops the effort with no further spend. Phase 2 (only if phase 1 passes): adopt the routing approach only if its fraction-met score is non-inferior to the strong-model-only baseline (margin of -0.05, clustered confidence interval) and its cost is no more than 0.6 times the strong-model-only baseline's cost.
risks: A false-accept under routing would ship an artifact capped at the weak model's quality ceiling outright; this is a live risk because a validator was already measured at a 25% false-accept rate in a related (though not identical) setup.
Running the full experimental grid before checker fidelity is established risks spending resources on a configuration that turns out to be doomed.

The written decision record was checked manually against every rule of the automated verification process rather than by actually running that automated check, because the automated check could not be executed in this session; independent confirmation is still needed before the decision is finalized.
assumptions: Quality under a routing/cascade mechanism is bounded by whichever model ultimately produces the output, not by a weaker model's ability to incorporate feedback, which is why this approach is treated as mechanistically distinct from the previously rejected iterative-coaching approach.
The prior rejection of a related but distinct approach does not by itself justify rejecting this approach, since the two were evaluated and decided as separate, independently measured cases.
There is no urgency to adopt the routing approach; the existing default approach continues to be used until the gate criteria are met.

expectations.why: Three options are enumerated; two are explicitly REJECTED with reasons — declining outright rejected because routing is mechanistically distinct from the prior iterative-coaching rejection, and running the full grid unconditionally rejected because a 25% false-accept rate was measured in a related setup.

Concrete falsifiable gates are stated: Phase 1 requires a checker variant at <=15% false-accept (UCB <=20%) or the effort stops; Phase 2 adopts only if fraction-met is non-inferior (margin -0.05) AND cost <=0.6x the strong-model baseline.

The load-bearing assumption in the key ('the battery had enough haiku-shippable tasks for routing to pay') is not surfaced. Stated assumptions cover quality-boundedness, non-generalization from the prior rejection, and no urgency; none address whether enough tasks are haiku-shippable for escalation to be rare enough to save cost.

The actual failure class was a cost failure (routing 1.04-1.33x sonnet-solo vs the <=0.6x target). The decision pre-registers exactly this dimension as a make-or-break Phase 2 criterion — cost no more than 0.6x the strong-model baseline — naming the cost-non-savings failure at the same threshold the outcome missed.

norm.decision: Extract the dispatcher console as a standalone service behind a thin API, keeping its tables in the shared database for now.
options: Extract the dispatcher console as a standalone service behind a thin API, tables staying in the shared database for now.

REJECTED: Full multi-quarter multi-service migration — its sole justification is a sales claim about procurement conversations that was asked to be substantiated twice with no evidence produced (no prospect name, no lost-deal note, no ticket); locking in infrastructure, on-call splits, and cross-team API contracts on an unverified secondhand claim was not accepted. The coupling map also undercuts pre-committing to a sequence: notifications (9 synchronous callers, 3 shared tables) and billing (regulated, 4 callers) are both multi-month entanglements, not extractions to schedule confidently before the pattern is proven even once.

REJECTED: Do nothing — it ignores the one problem that is actually verified firsthand, four engineers on the Ops Tools team genuinely blocked on the shared deploy train; no cost, but no benefit either.
norm.criterion: none

expectations.why: Three options enumerated: the chosen thin-API extraction plus two explicitly REJECTED alternatives (full multi-quarter migration; do nothing), each with a substantive reason (unverified sales claim / coupling entanglements; ignores the verified deploy-train blocker).

Overturned. The normalized decision's criterion field is explicitly 'none' and there are no stated risks or assumptions. The grader manufactured a reopen criterion out of rejection rationale: the 'prospect name / lost-deal note / ticket' phrasing is the evidence that was absent to justify the REJECTED full migration, not a stated condition to revisit or refute the CHOSEN thin-API extraction. 'Proven even once' gates future extractions and is vague (no concrete threshold for 'proven'). Nothing states what observation would refute or reopen the decision actually made. Indirect and requires charity — fails the falsifiable-criterion bar.

All three key expectations met: flags the demand/procurement claim as unverifiable (asked to substantiate twice, no evidence); chooses a reversible increment (thin API, tables kept in shared DB 'for now'); and records rejected alternatives with reasons.

Explicitly pushes back on the trap — the unverified secondhand claim was asked to be substantiated twice, produced no evidence, and was rejected as a basis for locking in infrastructure, rather than being adopted uncritically.

norm.decision: Harden the existing nightly sync script in place — adding retry with per-attempt telemetry, a single consistent-snapshot read of the source tables, and atomic publish via a single-PUT manifest pointer with a freshness marker — rather than adopting a workflow orchestration tool.
options: Harden the existing script in place: wrap the main routine in a bounded retry loop (3 attempts) that records each attempt (not just terminal status) and alerts on final failure; read all three source tables inside one REPEATABLE READ transaction so they share a consistent snapshot; write outputs to immutable run-scoped keys and publish atomically via a single PUT of a manifest file naming those keys plus the snapshot id and timestamp, with consumers resolving only through the manifest; add a freshness marker so consumers can detect stale data even if an alert goes unacknowledged. Keep the existing scheduler.
REJECTED: Leave the script unchanged (bare cron) — preserves silent failure on error and both timing-inconsistency windows (across the source reads, and across the separate output writes).

REJECTED: Adopt a workflow orchestration tool now — its per-task retry would re-run a failed table hours later and still produce a mixed-vintage result, so it does not actually fix the inconsistency issue used to justify it; it also imposes a permanent operational commitment (scheduler, metadata database, worker pool, ongoing upgrades) for a two-person team running a single linear job, justified mainly by speculative future optionality.
norm.criterion: Reopen consideration of the orchestration tool if any of: (1) five or more jobs develop genuine cross-job data dependencies (not just independently scheduled jobs); (2) measured from the new per-attempt telemetry, any source table retries on more than 10% of runs in a trailing month, or the alert fires more than once a quarter; (3) backfill/replay of past runs becomes routine (more than once a quarter); (4) job runtime approaches the scheduling window, requiring coordination across jobs.
risks: The derived views are refreshed against live source data at a later point in time and on a different data plane than the published snapshot, so consistency between the views and the published data is not guaranteed; resolving this would be a larger change than this decision covers.
No on-call rotation currently exists; the freshness marker mitigates the case where nobody is awake to see an alert, but establishing a rotation is a separate, unaddressed decision.
assumptions: The near-term trajectory does not involve an imminent expansion into many interdependent jobs; if on the order of a dozen dependency-coupled jobs are expected within roughly 6-12 months, this decision should be revisited early. Flagged as the weakest but load-bearing assumption.
A claim of 14 months with zero failures was asserted, but the available run log only covers and substantiates 30 days.
Cross-table data inconsistency actually matters to consumers of the derived views or to anyone reading the published data directly.
No failure alerting already exists elsewhere in the pipeline.

expectations.why: Three options enumerated; two are explicitly marked REJECTED with reasons (bare cron preserves silent failure/timing windows; orchestration tool's per-task retry produces mixed-vintage results and imposes permanent operational commitment).

Concrete falsifiable reopen criterion: e.g. >=5 jobs with cross-job dependencies, any source table retrying on >10% of runs in a trailing month, alert firing more than once a quarter, routine backfill/replay, or runtime approaching the scheduling window.

All three key expectations satisfied: rejects the framework with a stated reason; states scale/failure triggers that would reopen adoption; and surfaces maintenance burden (scheduler, metadata DB, worker pool, ongoing upgrades) as the load-bearing cost against a two-person team.

Does not anchor on the orchestration framework; explicitly rejects it, noting its per-task retry does not fix the inconsistency and it imposes a permanent operational commitment for a single linear job justified mainly by speculative optionality.

norm.decision: Adopt both prose rules together (a sync rule and a best-effort claim rule) to address concurrent duplicate work across sessions, rather than building a mechanized enforcement gate.
options: REJECTED: Prose sync rule only — it can only catch a collision after the other session has already merged or closed its work; it structurally cannot detect a still-in-flight concurrent sibling task.

REJECTED: Claim rule only — it would close the concurrent-sibling gap, but since the environment sometimes withholds write permission needed to post a claim, a mandatory claim requirement would silently fail exactly in the situations where it is most needed.

Both rules together, as prose (sync plus a best-effort, permission-conditional claim) — the two are complementary: sync catches the already-finished collision case and claim catches the in-flight case, so neither is optional if the goal is to actually close the race.

REJECTED: Full mechanization (e.g., a lock file or a pre-spend gate check) — there is no single choke point for "about to begin work" to instrument, building one would be substantial unbudgeted engineering, and the triggering incident was a single occurrence with a self-correcting outcome.
norm.criterion: A second duplicate-spend incident would escalate the approach from prose rules to full mechanization.
risks: The best-effort claim step can silently fail to register when write permission for posting a claim is withheld, leaving the in-flight collision case only partially covered.
Relying on prose rules rather than a mechanized gate leaves open the possibility of a repeat duplicate-spend incident if the prose approach proves insufficient.
assumptions: The cost of reversing course from a prose-only approach to mechanization later is near zero, making it safe to start with the lighter-weight option.
The triggering incident is treated as a single occurrence with a self-correcting outcome rather than evidence of a recurring, systemic problem.

expectations.why: Four options are enumerated; three are explicitly marked REJECTED each with a substantive reason (prose sync only can't catch in-flight siblings; claim only fails when write permission withheld; full mechanization has no single choke point and is unbudgeted). Clearly satisfies >=2 real options plus rejected alternatives with reasons.

The criterion field states a concrete, falsifiable reopen condition: 'A second duplicate-spend incident would escalate the approach from prose rules to full mechanization.' A repeat incident is an observable trigger that would revisit the decision.

Overturning the grader. The key's load-bearing assumption is 'prose is the right surface now' with the decisive parenthetical that its REOPEN-IF covered duplicate-spend ONLY — i.e. the bet that prose's SCOPE was the right net. The stated assumptions do not name this in substance. Assumption 1 is about reversibility/cost of switching later ('near zero, safe to start light') — a timing/escalation bet, not a claim that prose's surface adequately covers the coordination problem. Assumption 2 is about incident FREQUENCY (single, self-correcting occurrence), not surface appropriateness. Neither surfaces that the prose rule's scope might be too narrow to touch other coordination classes; reading assumption 1 as 'prose is the right starting surface' requires charity, and the mandate is to default met=false when the match is indirect. The assumption that actually broke was about scope, which is exactly the gap the stated assumptions leave unnamed.

The actual failure class was merge skew — two individually-green but distinct PRs merged seconds apart broke main ~18h later, a sibling-coordination class prose cannot touch. Every stated risk/assumption is scoped to DUPLICATE work: risks name only 'repeat duplicate-spend' recurrence and claim-step permission failure. None names the distinct-PR merge-skew / individually-green-changes-breaking-main failure mode, so the failure class that occurred is not anticipated.

norm.decision: Run the full pre-registered 4-arm by 8-task grid together (after the existing 1-rep pre-screen), rather than piloting the priciest arm alone with an early-stop-if-decidable rule.
options: Run the full pre-registered 4-arm x 8-task grid, using only the already-planned adaptive lever of adding reps or tasks where a confidence interval straddles the decision boundary, plus the already-decided deferral of the Opus-main arm.

REJECTED: Pilot the priciest arm (read as either Opus-solo, the quality ceiling and most expensive per call, or Tiered, whose up-to-3-cycle retries could make it priciest in aggregate tokens) first, stopping early if the decision gate is already decidable — rejected because the gate is a comparison between arms and cannot be evaluated from one arm's data, an unregistered mid-run stopping decision amounts to optional stopping that undermines the purpose of pre-registration, it would drop committed reporting owed on all four arms regardless of the gate's outcome, it saves little since the typical case still requires running the comparison arm anyway, and it tests new, more complex code on the most novel arm first instead of de-risking it on simpler, well-understood arms.
norm.criterion: Adoption gate: quality delta versus the Sonnet-solo baseline is at least -0.05 AND token usage is at most 0.7 times the Sonnet-solo baseline.
risks: Stopping early based on interim results without a pre-registered stopping rule for dropping an entire arm amounts to optional stopping, inflating the false-positive/false-negative rate.
Truncating arms could silently drop the committed reporting on placing all four arms on a quality-vs-cost frontier and on whether the tiered approach reaches Opus-solo quality at sub-Opus cost.
Running the new tiered-orchestration code first on the most complex, novel arm rather than shaking it out on simpler, well-understood arms first inverts the usual order for de-risking new code.
assumptions: The 'priciest arm' could mean either Opus-solo (quality ceiling, most expensive per call) or Tiered (whose retries, up to 3 cycles, could make it priciest in aggregate tokens).
The full grid is already lean by design, roughly 250-300 invocations total, having been pre-screened down to 8 tasks.
The tiered-orchestration approach requires only a small amount of new code.

expectations.why: Two real options are enumerated: the chosen full-grid run and a clearly labeled 'REJECTED' pilot-priciest-arm-with-early-stop alternative, with an explicit multi-part reason for rejection (gate is a cross-arm comparison, optional-stopping concern, dropped reporting, little savings, de-risking order).

The criterion field states a concrete, falsifiable adoption gate: quality delta vs Sonnet-solo baseline >= -0.05 AND token usage <= 0.7x the Sonnet-solo baseline — a testable condition that would determine adoption.

The key assumption that broke — that full-grid spend was needed for a valid verdict — is asserted only as a certainty inside the rejection rationale ('the gate is a comparison between arms and cannot be evaluated from one arm's data', 'still requires running the comparison arm anyway'), never surfaced in the assumptions/risks lists as a fallible belief. The stated assumptions cover only 'priciest arm' meaning, grid leanness (~250-300 invocations), and small code — none flag the load-bearing belief as questionable.

The actual failure — the gate was decidable after ~5 cells (~$0.60) so ~90% of the 216 runs were wasted spend — is not named. The stated risks (optional stopping inflating error rates, dropped committed reporting, testing new code on the novel arm) all argue the opposite direction; the decider explicitly maintained the gate could not be decided early, so the over-spend/early-decidability failure mode is anticipated in no risk, criterion, or assumption.

norm.decision: Execute the full pre-registered four-arm test grid (Haiku-solo, Sonnet-solo, Opus-solo, and Tiered, each across the same eight pre-screened tasks with three repetitions, run synchronized), applying the pre-registered sequential escalation only to repetitions and tasks rather than arms, and reject the proposal to pilot the most expensive arm (Opus-solo) first as an early-stopping shortcut.
options: Execute the full pre-registered grid as specified: four arms (Haiku-solo, Sonnet-solo, Opus-solo, Tiered) x eight tasks x three repetitions, run together/synchronized, with the pre-registered sequential escalation (add repetitions or tasks only where a confidence interval straddles the decision boundary, stop when unambiguous) applied only to repetitions/tasks, not arms.

REJECTED: Pilot the most expensive arm (Opus-solo) first and stop early if the gate is decidable — the adopt/reject rule depends only on Tiered and Sonnet-solo, so Opus-solo data alone can never resolve it at any sample size; the shortcut is therefore not a real option, saves no cost since the full grid must still run regardless, and would add an unregistered arm-level stopping rule after seeing interim results, risking inflated false-positive/false-negative risk on the headline result, plus a time-drift confound from staggering arms.

REJECTED: Pilot only the two arms the decision rule depends on (Tiered and Sonnet-solo) and treat Haiku-solo/Opus-solo as optional context — more coherent with the intent of economizing on spend, but it is a different design from what was pre-registered, and changing the design after seeing partial results is the optional-stopping failure mode the pre-registration exists to prevent; offered as a possible design for a future test, not adopted here.
norm.criterion: Adopt Tiered if delta(Tiered minus Sonnet-solo) is greater than or equal to -0.05 AND tokens(Tiered) is less than or equal to 0.7 times tokens(Sonnet-solo); this rule was pre-registered before running to avoid fishing for a favorable cut.
risks: Introducing an unregistered arm-level stopping rule after seeing interim results constitutes optional stopping, which inflates false-positive/false-negative risk on the headline delta/token result.
Staggering arms in time (running Opus-solo now, the others later) risks model/tooling drift between the pilot and the rest of the grid (grader version, task set, harness code).
assumptions: 'Most expensive arm' is taken to unambiguously mean Opus-solo, the highest per-token-cost model.
The overall spend (roughly 250-300 invocations) is already sized and implicitly approved at that scale, since the owner's pre-spend gating questions did not flag arm sequencing as an open issue.

expectations.why: The options array lists three real options: the adopted full-grid execution plus two explicitly REJECTED alternatives (pilot Opus-solo first; pilot only the two rule-dependent arms), each with substantive reasons (Opus-solo data can never resolve the gate / no cost saved / unregistered stopping rule; and the two-arm pilot is a post-hoc design change embodying optional-stopping).

The criterion field states a concrete, falsifiable, pre-registered acceptance rule: adopt Tiered iff delta(Tiered - Sonnet-solo) >= -0.05 AND tokens(Tiered) <= 0.7 x tokens(Sonnet-solo). It is a specific testable condition that determines the verdict.

The key assumption that broke is 'full-grid spend was needed for a valid verdict.' The decision holds this premise but does NOT surface it as an assumption open to question — it asserts it as fact ('the full grid must still run regardless') and uses it to reject the shortcut. The two stated assumptions concern 'most expensive arm = Opus-solo' and 'spend already sized/approved,' neither of which is the load-bearing assumption in substance. It is baked in as an unexamined premise, not surfaced.

The failure class is massive waste (gate decidable after ~5 cells / ~$0.60, but 216 ran, ~90% waste). No stated risk, criterion, or assumption names this; the risks cover optional stopping and time-drift only. The decision actively denies this failure mode, arguing the shortcut 'saves no cost since the full grid must still run regardless' — the opposite of anticipating the decidable-early waste that actually occurred.

norm.decision: Commission the pre-registered four-arm test comparing tiered multi-model execution to single-model execution now that its timing gate has been met and the test harness is reusable, keep the adoption decision itself gated on the pre-registered success rule, and pin the retry-cap, feedback, and logging specification before any spend so the retry loop cannot confound the token-cost metric under test.
options: Commission the pre-registered four-arm test now: run the comparison between tiered execution and single-model execution, while leaving the adoption decision itself gated on the pre-registered success rule rather than deciding adoption now.

On grader independence: accept the residual bias from having the same model grade its own output rather than requiring an independent grader; the test's internal validator sees only the task, never the expected answers, and passing results receive an additional adversarial check pass.

On environment controls: carry over the existing isolation controls (clean configuration, no installed extensions, neutral framing) but do not withhold implementation tools, since using them is the task itself; record which basis was used for the run.

On retry handling: cap total attempts at three, with the first attempt counted as cycle one; on cap exhaustion, grade whatever the final cycle produced as-is instead of having the primary model take over implementation, since a takeover would silently add an undefined fifth condition to the test; use a fresh, non-resumed worker each cycle, passing the prior cycle's verdict verbatim as feedback, and log each cycle's verdict in a fixed format alongside cost data.

REJECTED: Defer commissioning the test — leaves the underlying pending assumption stuck on its named signal and the tiered-execution idea asserted indefinitely, a state the governing prior decision forbids.

REJECTED: Leave the retry-cap and feedback-loop specification to whoever implements the test — unpinned specifications default silently, and here the default would confound the token-cost metric under test with no cheap way to catch it after the fact.

REJECTED: Add a third, independent grading model now — premature machinery ahead of demonstrated need, given the existing accepted self-grading residual plus adversarial-check approach already covers it.
norm.criterion: Adoption of tiered execution is accepted only if, once the test reports, the score delta versus single-model execution is at least -0.05 and total tokens are at most 0.7x the single-model cost; the accepted self-grading-bias assumption is reopened if a non-Claude/human spot-check on a boundary case diverges materially from the self-graded verdict.
risks: A validator-fidelity threat: the internal per-cycle validator could false-accept or false-reject relative to the final grader, distorting the measured results.
Self-grading bias between the tiered approach and single-model execution could dominate the measured delta, since it is structurally unresolvable with only three model tiers available as executors.
An undefined retry or feedback loop, or unpinned model defaults, could confound the total-tokens metric under test, forcing a costly rerun.
Falling back to a primary-model takeover of implementation on cap exhaustion would silently create an undefined fifth test condition and void the four-arm test design.
The new orchestration script could balloon beyond a thin reuse of existing scripts into a general orchestrator, becoming spend outside what this decision authorizes.
assumptions: The self-grading residual is assumed not to dominate the measured delta between the tiered approach and single-model execution; this cannot be independently verified with only three model tiers, so it is accepted, subject to reopening if a non-Claude/human spot-check on a boundary case diverges materially.
The estimated invocation count for the test is assumed to include grading and adversarial-check passes, not only executor calls; this is to be verified before spend against existing benchmark data.
The new orchestration script is assumed to remain a thin reuse of existing scripts rather than becoming a general orchestrator; this is to be checked when the resulting change is proposed.

This decision is assumed to commission the test without resolving the underlying pending question; resolution is assumed to land in a later record once the test reports, with a fresh signal named at that time if the results carry no relevant signal.

expectations.why: The options array lists multiple real options plus three explicitly REJECTED alternatives each with a reason (e.g. 'REJECTED: Defer commissioning the test — leaves the underlying pending assumption stuck...', 'REJECTED: Add a third, independent grading model now — premature machinery...'). Clearly >=2 options including rejected alternatives with reasons.

The criterion is concrete and falsifiable: adoption accepted only if score delta vs single-model is >= -0.05 AND total tokens <= 0.7x single-model cost; and the self-grading-bias assumption is reopened if a non-Claude/human spot-check on a boundary case diverges materially. Both a gating acceptance condition and a reopen trigger are specified.

The key load-bearing assumption that broke is 'plan+validate overhead < cheap-worker savings at this task granularity' (i.e., that tiered execution is actually net-cheaper). This is NOT stated in the assumptions or risks. The listed assumptions cover self-grading residual, invocation-count accounting, thin orchestration script, and commissioning-without-resolving; the risks cover validator fidelity, self-grading bias, retry-loop confounding the token metric, takeover creating a fifth arm, and script scope creep. The decision treats net cost savings as the thing under test rather than surfacing the overhead-vs-savings tradeoff as an explicit assumption/risk, so the substance is absent from the assumptions/risks arrays.

The key failure_class combines a cost blowup (tokens far above the bar) and validator false-accepts. Risk #1 directly names the validator-fidelity failure: 'the internal per-cycle validator could false-accept or false-reject relative to the final grader.' The cost-blowup component is also engaged: the criterion gates adoption on total tokens <= 0.7x and Risk #3 flags confounding of the total-tokens metric forcing a costly rerun, anticipating that cost may fail to come in low. The actual failure mode is named in substance.

norm.decision: Adopt a prose rule requiring a fetch of the repository and re-read of an issue's/PR's live state before executing a backlog issue and again before each expensive nested batch of work (not only at session start/end), rather than building a claim/lock protocol, in response to a backlog issue being executed twice across parallel sessions at a cost of roughly $5-8.
options: Sync-before-spend as a prose rule, triggered per expensive unit of work: fetch and re-read the issue's/PR's live state before executing the issue and before each expensive nested batch of work, abort if it turns out to be closed, and re-check again before the final push.

REJECTED: A claim/lock protocol (marking an issue as in-progress) as the primary defense, whether alone or combined with the prose rule — it is not enforceable within the repository because it depends on issue-write permission that external dispatch configuration sometimes withholds, so it would silently fail for exactly the sessions most likely to need it, it introduces its own stuck-lock failure modes, and it is premature machinery to build and maintain after only one incident.

REJECTED: A sync rule limited to session-start/session-end brackets (the literal wording of the originating issue) — it misses the mid-execution window where the actual collision occurred, since a start-of-session check would have seen the issue as genuinely still open and an end-of-session check only prevents shipping a redundant result after the cost has already been spent.
REJECTED: Do nothing — leaves the underlying failure mode open at near-zero cost to address it.
norm.criterion: Reopen the decision if a second duplicate-execution incident occurs despite the prose rule, in which case a claim/lock mechanism should be built (first auditing whether external dispatch configurations even permit writing such a marker); also reopen if dispatch-prompt files begin to exist within the repository, since that would make a claim protocol enforceable and worth re-weighing.
risks: The claim protocol depends on issue-write permission that external dispatch configuration sometimes withholds, so it would silently fail for exactly the sessions most likely to need it.
A claim/lock mechanism introduces its own stuck-lock failure modes, such as a crashed session or a false claim.
Whether sessions will actually follow the prose rule going forward is unverifiable.
The prose rule is not a true structural safeguard; real prevention would require serialization at the dispatch/launch-configuration level, which is outside the repository's control.

The environment used to prepare this decision had no configured remote, so a check for a colliding identifier against the remote's main branch or open branches could only run partially; this needs to be reconfirmed before merging.
The single incident being self-caught and low-cost was fortunate rather than evidence that duplicated work is generally cheap.
assumptions: No dispatch-prompt file exists within the repository — verified by search, which found only one issue-reference file and two benchmark readme files, meaning the claim marker cannot be enforced within the repository and issue-write permission lives in external session-launch configuration.

An existing repository rule requiring a fetch before ranging, branching, and retrospect activity is scoped separately from issue-execution spend, so the new rule extends rather than duplicates it — verified by reading the existing rule.
The new rule's text stays within the overall character budget of the document it is added to — checked and, after an initial draft exceeded the limit, fixed by shortening the added text.

expectations.why: The options array lists the chosen prose rule plus three explicitly REJECTED alternatives, each with a reason (claim/lock protocol — unenforceable/premature; session-start/end-only sync — misses mid-execution window; do nothing — leaves failure open). Multiple real options with rejection reasons.

The criterion field states a concrete, falsifiable reopen condition: reopen if a second duplicate-execution incident occurs despite the rule (then build a claim/lock mechanism), and also reopen if dispatch-prompt files begin to exist in the repository. Both are observable triggers.

The load-bearing assumption is that prose is the right surface now. The risks surface this in substance: 'The prose rule is not a true structural safeguard; real prevention would require serialization at the dispatch/launch-configuration level, which is outside the repository's control,' plus 'Whether sessions will actually follow the prose rule ... is unverifiable' — directly questioning whether prose is the adequate surface.

The actual failure class was a distinct sibling-coordination mode — two individually-green, DIFFERENT PRs merged ~7s apart causing merge skew that broke main ~18h later. Nothing in the risks/criterion/assumptions names merge skew or two separate PRs colliding at integration time; all coordination content is framed around duplicate execution of the SAME issue and launch serialization, not merge-time collision of different PRs.

norm.decision: Retire the v1 geocoding API on a firm dated sunset (2027-02-28, with staged notices at T-180/90/30/7), closing the migration-cost information gap through per-key instrumentation and forced surfacing of silent usage rather than a guessed runway, with a v1-compatibility shim offered only as a conditional, tightly gated opt-in accelerator.
options: Dated retirement of the v1 code path with a firm sunset date of 2027-02-28, staged notices, cost-gap closure via instrumentation and forced surfacing, per-account early lapse, and a conditional opt-in compatibility shim — adopted as the plan of record.

REJECTED: Indefinite retention (keep v1 running until complaints stop) — traffic is flat and non-declining, so this criterion would never terminate and amounts to an unbounded tax on v2 users to subsidize the tail.

REJECTED: 60-day sunset — would break one account's live dispatch integration with zero migration-cost data in hand, and relies on a survivorship error that treats the small number of vocal complaining accounts as representative of risk instead of the larger silent, unmeasured tail.

Adapter/shim that serves v1's shape over the v2 engine — not rejected outright but permitted only if a timeboxed probe clears a strict per-customer, per-percentile tolerance signed off by the account, and only as a disclosed, opt-in, continuously parity-gated bridge; dropped entirely if it cannot meet disclosure, continuous-gating, and signed-tolerance requirements.
norm.criterion: Reopen if a full 30-day traffic re-pull shows v1 volume outside 82k-94k requests or a share not approximately 0.40% flat; reopen if the cost-gap probe shows v1-to-v2 divergence is shape-only within signed tolerance, in which case the compatibility shim should be evaluated on its own separate decision; reopen if a forced brownout surfaces an account whose measured critical path exceeds the 2027-02-28 date, in which case the date is extended outward for that account only.
risks: A compatibility shim could produce a silent false-positive (appearing compatible when it is not) if deployed without adequate safeguards.
The compatibility tolerance for judging whether the shim is acceptable was left undefined until tightened in this decision.
Direct evidence (bugfixes for v1 and v2 having to be written and tested twice) contradicts the assumption that v1-to-v2 divergence is shape-only, undermining the case for the shim.
A shorter (60-day) timeline was less honest about the true migration effort than the dated approach.
An aggregate traffic-volume floor for early access retirement would have been unreachable or produced inverted incentives; replaced with per-account lapse criteria.
Migration-cost estimates gathered only from accounts that respond to outreach are subject to survivorship bias, since only self-selected responders are counted.
The original traffic figure was a mislabeled 14-day total misread as a full month; the corrected run-rate is roughly double (about 88k/month vs. the originally cited ~41k).
No per-customer migration-cost estimate exists yet, which is the information needed to properly size the transition runway.

Several named accounts oppose the sunset for different reasons: one is hardcoded to v1's response shape in a live dispatch system, one reports no bandwidth this year, and one has bursty but load-bearing nightly traffic.
There may be a silent, currently unmeasured tail of v1 accounts whose usage is load-bearing and would only become visible through forced brownouts or header-based deprecation signals.
assumptions: The compatibility-shim option assumes divergence between v1 and v2 is shape-only (structural/format differences) rather than behavioral, despite evidence of duplicated bugfix effort suggesting real behavioral divergence — identified as the single most load-bearing and most likely wrong assumption underlying the decision.
v1 traffic is assumed to be flat and non-declining, based on a 14-day sample corrected to an estimated run-rate of about 88,000 requests per month at roughly a 0.40% share of total traffic.
The firm sunset date is assumed to self-correct outward on new evidence and not strand any account whose usage is later surfaced, rather than needing to be fixed in advance.

expectations.why: Options list enumerates the adopted dated-retirement plan plus two explicitly REJECTED alternatives each with reasons: indefinite retention (traffic flat/non-declining, unbounded tax on v2 users) and the 60-day sunset (breaks live dispatch integration, survivorship error). Also a conditionally-gated shim option. Clearly >=2 real options with a rejected alternative and reason.

The criterion field states concrete falsifiable reopen conditions: reopen if a 30-day traffic re-pull shows v1 volume outside 82k-94k requests or share not ~0.40% flat; reopen if the cost-gap probe shows shape-only divergence within signed tolerance; reopen if a brownout surfaces an account whose critical path exceeds the sunset date. Concrete, testable conditions.

All three key expectations are met: (a) weighs the base-rate metric over anecdotes — 60-day rejection explicitly calls out treating vocal complainers as representative vs the silent tail; (b) falsifiable sunset criterion with date (2027-02-28), usage thresholds (82k-94k / ~0.40%), and notice period (T-180/90/30/7); (c) names the migration-cost-to-affected-users assumption ('No per-customer migration-cost estimate exists yet', closing the migration-cost information gap). Well over the 2-of-3 bar.

The decision explicitly pushes back on the loud-minority bait: the 60-day rejection cites a 'survivorship error that treats the small number of vocal complaining accounts as representative of risk instead of the larger silent, unmeasured tail,' and the plan closes the info gap via instrumentation and forced surfacing rather than reacting to the three complaints. It does not anchor on the vocal minority; it foregrounds the 0.40% base-rate metric and the unmeasured tail.

norm.decision: Adopt keeping the existing cron-based approach and add a small retry-with-backoff and alert wrapper, rejecting the option to adopt Conductor for now.
options: Keep cron, add a small retry-with-backoff and alert wrapper
REJECTED: Adopt Conductor — rejected for now
norm.criterion: Revisit the decision if the job count or coupling increases, if repeated manual intervention is needed, or if there is a recurring backfill need.

expectations.why: Two options are enumerated: 'Keep cron, add retry-with-backoff and alert wrapper' and 'REJECTED: Adopt Conductor'. The rejection reason is thin ('for now') but the decision defers the framework in favor of keeping the simpler existing approach, with the reopen criterion implicitly supplying the rationale (current scale/coupling doesn't justify it).

Concrete, falsifiable reopen criterion is stated: revisit if job count or coupling increases, if repeated manual intervention is needed, or if there is a recurring backfill need. These are observable conditions that would refute/revisit the decision.

Overturned. Of the 3 key expectations, only one is clearly satisfied: the scale/failure reopen trigger (#2). The 'defers with a stated reason' sub-point (#1) is thin — 'for now' is a temporal hedge, not a stated reason, and crediting it requires charitably inferring the rationale from the reopen criterion. The load-bearing cost sub-point (#3 — maintenance burden) is entirely absent: the decision never surfaces the operational/maintenance overhead of a framework relative to a 40-line script. Reaching the 2-of-3 threshold depends on charity for #1 while the actual point of the scenario (#3) is missed, so it fails the substantive-majority test.

Correctly discounts the gold-plating bait: instead of adopting the attractive workflow-orchestration framework for a small nightly job, it keeps the existing cron script and adds only a minimal retry/alert wrapper, explicitly rejecting Conductor. This is the right proportionate response to the trap.

norm.decision: Set the free-tier request cap at 300 requests per day per API key.
options: REJECTED: 1,000 requests/day — this number was under consideration only because the metering infrastructure already supports it, not because it was a reasoned choice; at that level the cap would never bind for any observed user (fleet average is about 94-106 requests per key per day), making the free tier effectively unmetered and defeating the goal of the cap acting as a lever toward paid-tier conversion.

REJECTED: about 150 requests/day, near the observed mean — the available data has only fleet-level daily totals, not per-key data; the tight day-over-day average is exactly what averaging over roughly 40 keys would produce even if some individual keys run at 250-400 requests/day, a tail invisible in this data; capping near the mean risks throttling ordinary users on a normal heavier day before the paid tier has proven anything, for no offsetting benefit.

300 requests/day per key — roughly 3x the fleet-average day, which absorbs normal variance; well under a third of the infrastructure ceiling, which still constrains actual heavy users, the population expected to convert to paid; and low enough to read as a deliberate tier boundary rather than an artifact of infrastructure capacity.
norm.criterion: Lower the cap if per-key p90/p95 usage data, once logged, shows 300 is not actually binding on anyone, or if conversions stay near zero and free users never encounter the cap; raise the cap if launch produces a wave of complaints from clearly ordinary usage hitting 300; handle isolated legitimate high-volume cases (e.g., CI/OSS) through an allowlist rather than moving the global cap; revisit the decision in 60-90 days once real conversion data exists.
risks: The current usage data is fleet-level only and cannot distinguish a scenario where all keys use about 100 requests/day from one where most use about 80 and a few use about 400, since per-key percentile data has not yet been logged.
Setting the cap too low risks throttling ordinary users during normal usage variance before the paid tier has had a chance to demonstrate value.
Setting the cap too high, e.g. near the infrastructure ceiling, risks making the free tier effectively unmetered, removing the intended incentive toward paid-tier conversion.
assumptions: Fleet-average usage is approximately 94-106 requests per key per day across the observed 30-day period.
Some individual keys may be running at 250-400 requests/day, a tail that would be invisible in the tight fleet-level average produced by averaging across roughly 40 keys.
The cap is intended to function as a lever toward conversion to a paid tier.

expectations.why: Enumerates three options: rejected 1,000/day (infra-artifact, non-binding, defeats conversion lever), rejected ~150/day near mean (throttles ordinary users on heavy days), and the chosen 300/day — each rejected alternative carries an explicit reason.

Criterion is concrete and falsifiable: lower if per-key p90/p95 data shows 300 non-binding or conversions stay near zero with users never hitting the cap; raise on a wave of complaints from ordinary usage; revisit in 60-90 days once real conversion data exists.

All three key expectations satisfied: (a) refuses to anchor on the already-built 1000/day metering capacity, calling it an artifact of infra not a reasoned choice; (b) states a falsifiable conversion-rate revisit trigger; (c) names conversion as the load-bearing assumption ('cap intended to function as a lever toward conversion').

Directly pushes back on the sunk-cost bait: the 1,000/day option is rejected precisely because it 'was under consideration only because the metering infrastructure already supports it, not because it was a reasoned choice,' and would leave the free tier effectively unmetered — explicit discounting of the trap.

norm.decision: Keep the existing cron script for the job and adopt nothing new (neither a retry/alert wrapper nor a full workflow orchestration platform) at this time.
options: Keep the cron script and adopt nothing new — the run history shows a clean success record with a tight 5.7-6.2 minute runtime band, so there is no observed reliability problem to solve.

REJECTED: Add a retry/alert wrapper around the cron job — with zero observed failures in 14 months there is no evidence of the transient-failure class this would guard against; it is speculative complexity against a hypothetical, not an observed, failure mode, and cron's current run-it-and-page-on-nonzero-exit already gives correct failure signaling for a single job.

REJECTED: Adopt a full workflow orchestration platform (DAG UI, lineage, backfill orchestration, plugin ecosystem) — the job is a single 41-line task with no fan-out, no cross-job dependencies, and no backfill need, so the platform's coordination features answer a problem this job doesn't have; running it would mean a standing service (scheduler, metadata database, workers, upgrade treadmill) imposing a permanent operational cost on a two-person team.
norm.criterion: Revisit the decision if any one of the following occurs: (1) coupling/scale — the number of jobs grows to roughly a dozen or more, or real inter-job dependencies appear (one job must follow another, shared backfill windows); (2) failure rate — the job fails twice within any rolling 90-day window for reasons bare cron plus paging cannot handle (transient blips a retry would fix, or failures needing backfill rather than a simple rerun), using two data points rather than one to avoid over-reacting to a single fluke; (3) backfill need — reprocessing historical dates becomes a routine need rather than a trivial manual rerun. If only the failure-rate trigger fires (no coupling, no backfill need), the correct response is adding a retry/alert wrapper, not moving straight to the full orchestration platform; only the scale/coupling or backfill triggers justify adopting the full platform.

The team is two people, for whom operating a standing orchestration service would be a significant, permanent operational burden regardless of need.
The job remains a single, self-contained task with no fan-out, no cross-job dependencies, and no backfill requirement.

risks: Checker false-acceptance of low-quality outputs during escalation: the closest available analog already showed a 25% false-accept rate, against a measured quality gap of 0.16-0.28 (fraction of criteria met) between the lower- and higher-capability models, which alone is plausibly enough to sink non-inferiority if the same rate holds for the new routing approach.
assumptions: The closest available analog (a validator's false-accept behavior from a prior parallel run) is representative of how a routing checker would behave in the new approach.
Outputs and grades already committed from the prior study are adequate for scoring candidate checkers in the initial phase without any new model-generation spend.

expectations.why: The options array lists three real options, two explicitly marked REJECTED with substantive reasons (decline-outright rejected because it assumes rather than measures; run-full-grid rejected because a 25% false-accept analog against a 0.16-0.28 quality gap risks wasted spend). Clear enumeration of >=2 options including rejected alternatives with reasons.

The criterion is concrete and falsifiable: initial phase must clear a <=15% false-accept ceiling to unlock phase two; phase two applies non-inferiority within -0.05 (clustered CI) and cost materially below the higher-cost baseline; otherwise do-not-adopt with no further spend. These are testable pass/fail conditions.

The key load-bearing assumption is that the battery had enough haiku-shippable tasks for routing to pay. The stated assumptions concern only (a) the analog validator being representative of the routing checker and (b) prior outputs/grades being adequate to score checkers. Neither addresses whether enough tasks are cheaply shippable for routing to save cost; that assumption is not surfaced in substance.

The actual failure class was faithful checkers escalating ~75%, making every routing variant 1.04-1.33x sonnet-solo cost (a cost failure via over-escalation). The stated risk names the opposite mechanism: checker false-ACCEPTANCE of low-quality outputs sinking non-inferiority (a quality failure). The cost criterion is a generic pass bar, not an anticipation of the escalation-driven cost blowup. The failure mode is not named in substance.

norm.decision: Authorize only a checker-fidelity pilot for the proposed routing mechanism (checker evaluates the 24 retained haiku-solo artifacts, no new task execution) with a pre-registered false-accept kill gate, and require any full adoption to clear both that gate and the existence of a real consumption point for a winning result.
options: Run the checker-fidelity pilot only, reusing the existing hermetic executor and blind-grade process, comparing at least two checker variants against the 24 retained haiku-solo artifacts, with the kill gate pre-registered before any checker call and adoption double-gated on the gate passing and a consumption point existing (accepted)

REJECTED: Authorize the full/adopt arm now or on pilot success alone — no consumption point exists for a winning result, so a positive verdict could not be acted on, and it would fund further spend without proof of value

REJECTED: Decline the pilot entirely — this would forgo cheap information on a mechanism variant not previously tested, when the only cost is checker calls on already-retained artifacts and even a null result would be informative
REJECTED: Build an escalation harness first to create a landing pad for a future positive result — premature, since it builds infrastructure before the mechanism has demonstrated it earns one
norm.criterion: A checker variant must beat approximately a 10-15% false-accept rate on the retained artifacts, with this bar pre-registered before any checker call is made; if no variant clears it, the result is recorded as a null and the effort stops. Separately, any move to full adoption is gated on both this bar being cleared and a real consumption point (a live or committed per-task escalation mechanism) existing to use a winning result.
risks: Authorizing a full-scale run on the strength of pilot success alone would fund further spend without a place for the result to be used, since no consumption mechanism currently exists
The pilot's low cost depends on the retained artifacts being free of task or rubric drift since they were captured; this dependency is called out as the weakest link in the plan
Clearing the false-accept bar is considered a priori unlikely, based on a prior related measurement of a 25% false-accept rate
Building an escalation harness before it is needed, in order to create a landing pad, would be premature and add unneeded process machinery
assumptions: The 24 retained haiku-solo artifacts (and their summary) are valid inputs for testing checker fidelity without re-running the original task, i.e. no task or rubric drift has occurred since they were captured; presence of the artifacts was confirmed, but a drift check against the original run's metadata is still owed before the pilot spends any resources

No live or planned mechanism exists today that would dynamically escalate a task from one model tier to another on a per-task basis, so a winning routing configuration currently has nothing to plug into; this was checked against the relevant configuration source and a search for any such orchestration mechanism
