A checker (a model-based judge or a deterministic checklist) will beat the roughly 10-15% false-accept bar on the retained artifacts — this is not yet known and is the specific falsifiable outcome the pilot is meant to measure

expectations.why: The options array lists four real options: one accepted (run the checker-fidelity pilot only) and three explicitly REJECTED, each with a stated reason (authorize full/adopt now — no consumption point; decline pilot entirely — forgoes cheap information; build escalation harness first — premature). Clearly satisfies >=2 options plus a rejected alternative with a reason.

The criterion states a concrete, falsifiable bar pre-registered before any checker call: a checker variant must beat ~10-15% false-accept rate on the retained artifacts; if no variant clears it, the result is recorded as a null and the effort stops. This is a specific condition that would refute/stop the effort.

The key load-bearing assumption is 'the battery had enough haiku-shippable tasks for routing to pay.' None of the stated assumptions/risks address the base rate of haiku-shippable tasks. The stated assumptions concern artifact drift, absence of a consumption/escalation mechanism, and whether a checker clears the false-accept bar — the checker-fidelity dimension, not whether enough tasks are haiku-shippable for routing to pay off.

The actual failure class is that every checker variant cost 1.04-1.33x sonnet-solo (vs the <=0.6x needed) because faithful checkers escalate ~75% of tasks — a cost/escalation-rate failure. The decision deliberately narrows the pilot to checker fidelity (false-accept rate) and defers cost/escalation entirely; its risks flag only that the false-accept bar is a priori unlikely to clear (25% prior). It never names the escalation-rate-driven cost blowup — indeed the checkers succeeded on fidelity but failed on cost, the mode the decision did not anticipate.

norm.decision: Extract dispatcher_console into its own service behind a thin API while leaving its tables in the shared database, treating this as a reversible first increment rather than a commitment to a platform-wide migration, and reject both the full migration and doing nothing.
options: Extract dispatcher_console as a single service behind a thin API with its tables left in the shared database, treated as a reversible first increment rather than a full commitment
REJECTED: Full platform-wide migration to multiple services — makes an expensive, slow-to-unwind commitment (infrastructure, on-call rotations, cross-team API contracts) based on unverified demand
REJECTED: Do nothing — the release-train blockage is a real, present cost to a four-person team, and a cheap reversible fix exists
norm.criterion: Success/continuation criterion: over a 6-8 week trial (minimum 5 releases) after cutover, the approach is refuted if fewer than 60% of the blocked team's releases ship without waiting on the shared deploy train — in that case, revert (a roughly 1-week routing change) and do not proceed toward the larger migration. Reopen criterion: reconsider the full migration if one concrete artifact (a named prospect, a lost-deal note, or a procurement ticket) ties a deal to the platform-migration question, or if the trial above passes and a second team independently presents a justified case for another extraction; absent either, the decision stands.
risks: If the blocked team's pain is actually dominated by shared-schema migration coordination rather than the shared deploy artifact, the extraction ships a distributed monolith and buys little relief.

A hidden writer to the event table, or the extracted service's access to other shared tables turning out not to be read-only, would convert the intended thin seam into a data-coupling problem and change the cost of reversal.

The enterprise demand cited as the main driver for the full migration is unverified — asked about twice, no concrete supporting artifact (named prospect, lost-deal note, or procurement ticket) has been produced.
assumptions: A code-only extraction that leaves the database shared will actually relieve the blocked team's release-time blockage, i.e. that their pain stems from the shared deploy artifact rather than shared-schema coordination.

The prior architectural trace of the system (dated roughly a month before this decision) is assumed accurate but not yet reverified against current code — specifically that the write target used by dispatcher_console has no external readers and that its access to other shared tables (bookings/carriers) is read-only; this must be confirmed before cutover.

The extracted component has zero inbound synchronous internal callers, making it a low-coupling seam whose reversal is a simple routing change, in contrast to more entangled components in the system that were excluded from consideration for this first cut.

expectations.why: Options list has three: the chosen dispatcher_console extraction plus two REJECTED alternatives each with a stated reason (full migration = expensive/unverified-demand commitment; do nothing = real present cost with a cheap reversible fix available).

Criterion is concretely falsifiable: over a 6-8 week trial (min 5 releases), refuted if <60% of blocked team's releases ship without the shared deploy train -> revert; plus explicit reopen conditions (a named artifact tying a deal to migration, or trial-pass + a second justified extraction case).

All three scenario expectations satisfied: flags enterprise demand as unverified (risks + assumptions), chooses a reversible increment and names reversal cost ('roughly 1-week routing change'), and records rejected alternatives with reasons.

The planted trap (unverifiable 'users demand microservices' premise) is explicitly discounted: risks note the enterprise demand 'is unverified — asked about twice, no concrete supporting artifact... has been produced,' and the full migration is rejected precisely for resting on unverified demand rather than adopted.

norm.decision: Proceed with the routing-cascade experiment, but gate the expensive full-grid run behind a near-zero-cost checker-fidelity pilot on already-committed data, only proceeding to the full grid if the checker clears a false-accept threshold.
options: REJECTED: Decline the experiment outright as already settled by the prior finding against tiered execution generally — the prior finding (iterating a weaker implementer against a stronger critic converges to the implementer's ceiling) does not apply to routing, because routing has no iteration: a single attempt is gated by a checker, and on rejection the whole task moves to the stronger model; treating the question as already closed conflates two distinct mechanisms.

REJECTED: Run the full experimental grid (8 tasks x 3 arms x 3 reps) immediately without first checking checker viability — a related prior measurement already found a validator false-accepting 25% (6/24) of final artifacts; under routing there is no correction step, so a false-accept would ship a lower-quality artifact as final, and running the full grid before checking this would repeat a previously-identified mistake of paying for a result likely to be sunk by a variable that was never measured first.

Run a two-stage gated approach: first, a cheap checker-fidelity pilot using already-committed data (no new executor spend, only checker calls) to measure false-accept rate; only if that clears a set threshold, run the full grid with pre-registered adoption criteria — CHOSEN.
norm.criterion: Stage-1 gate: the best-performing checker must achieve a false-accept rate of 15% or lower, or the experiment stops with a null result recorded and no further spend. Stage-2 (only if Stage 1 clears) adoption bar: fraction of tasks meeting criteria must be non-inferior to the single-model baseline (margin -0.05, clustered confidence interval) and measured median cost must be no more than 0.6x the single-model baseline cost.
risks: A false-accept under routing ships a lower-capability-floor artifact as the final result, because routing (unlike iterative approaches) has no correction step to catch it.
Running the full-cost experiment before checking checker viability risks paying for a result on a variable (checker false-accept rate) that could sink the outcome before it is even measured.
assumptions: The already-committed benchmark data (executor outputs and their independently graded ground truth) is valid and sufficient to measure checker false-accept rate without any new executor spend.

Routing's failure surface (checker fidelity on a single gated attempt) is mechanistically distinct from the iterative-coaching failure surface addressed by the prior decision, making this a legitimate new question rather than a re-litigation of a settled one.

expectations.why: The options array enumerates three real options: two REJECTED alternatives each with a substantive reason (decline as settled by prior finding; run full grid immediately) plus the CHOSEN gated two-stage approach. Multiple rejected alternatives with reasons clearly satisfy this.

Concrete falsifiable conditions are pre-registered: Stage-1 gate requires checker false-accept rate <=15% else the experiment stops with a null result; Stage-2 adoption requires non-inferior quality (margin -0.05, clustered CI) and median cost <=0.6x baseline. These are concrete pass/fail thresholds that would halt or refute the decision.

The load-bearing assumption in the key is that the battery had enough haiku-shippable tasks for routing to pay (a task-mix / low-escalation assumption). The stated assumptions concern only (a) the committed data being valid/sufficient to measure checker false-accept and (b) routing's failure surface being mechanistically distinct from iterative coaching. Neither the assumptions nor risks address whether enough tasks are cheap-model-shippable. The decision in fact implicitly assumed the opposite (that routing would pay). Not surfaced in substance.

Overturned. The failure class is escalation-driven cost blowup: only 6/24 haiku cells were shippable, so even a faithful checker escalates ~75% and routing costs 1.04-1.33x sonnet-solo — a STRUCTURAL failure, not a checker defect (recorded ADR:28-31). The decision's substantive anticipation points the opposite way: both stated risks, both assumptions, and the primary Stage-1 gate all target the checker being too LENIENT (false-accepts shipping bad artifacts). Worse, the Stage-1 false-accept gate would have SELECTED FOR the faithful checkers that caused the failure (the mechanized checker's 8% false-accept clears the 15% bar). The decision never names the escalation mechanism or the possibility that a task mix short on haiku-shippable work destroys the cost case. The lone element touching the actual failure variable — the Stage-2 cost bar (<=0.6x) — is a passive boilerplate adoption threshold present in any cost experiment; it measures the outcome but does not name the failure MODE (escalation-driven cost blowup) in substance. Crediting it requires treating a success threshold as active failure-anticipation while ignoring that every stated risk/assumption anticipated the opposite failure. That is the charity the refuter strips.

norm.decision: Mechanize the sync half of the issue-preflight process now by building a script and a documentation rule, while deferring the claim/comment protocol to a future cycle.
options: Build a preflight script that runs a fetch of the main branch, checks the issue's state, and searches merged pull requests/commits referencing the issue, since a plain fetch alone would not have surfaced the relevant prior pull request.

Add a rule to the project documentation mandating the preflight step before starting execution on a backlog issue, and again immediately before the final push, replacing a remembered multi-step ritual with one callable command.
House the related lesson about foreground waits on long child runs together with this same implementation rather than as a separate record, keeping one home for it.

REJECTED: Build the claim/comment protocol this cycle — it requires write permission that is not always available, and the incident as documented reflects staleness accumulated during a session's run rather than a confirmed case of two sessions starting simultaneously, so building conditional infrastructure for an unconfirmed failure mode would be premature.

Revisit and add the claim/comment protocol only if a future incident shows two sessions colliding despite both having run the preflight step, which would confirm a real race condition rather than staleness.
norm.criterion: If a future incident shows two sessions colliding despite both having run the preflight step, proving it is a real race rather than staleness, then add the claim/comment protocol.
risks: Building infrastructure for the claim/comment protocol now, before a failure mode is confirmed, would itself be premature and unnecessary machinery.
The claim/comment protocol depends on write permission to the issue tracker that is not always granted, so it cannot be made a hard rule.
The checks on the produced record were verified by hand rather than by running the checking tool, because the environment blocked running it, leaving a residual risk that a check was missed.
The script and documentation edit described as deferred work still need a tracking item opened, but the environment lacked confirmed access to create one, leaving that step for the requester to do.
assumptions: The incident being addressed is understood as staleness accumulated over the course of a session's run (the session synced at the start, then went stale), not a confirmed case of two sessions beginning simultaneously.
Manual verification against the checking tool's known rules is treated as an adequate substitute for actually running that tool, given the environment prevented running it directly.

expectations.why: The options list contains multiple real options plus an explicitly REJECTED alternative ('Build the claim/comment protocol this cycle') with stated reasons: it requires write permission not always available and the incident reflects staleness rather than a confirmed simultaneous-start race, making it premature.

The criterion field states a concrete, falsifiable reopen condition: 'If a future incident shows two sessions colliding despite both having run the preflight step, proving it is a real race rather than staleness, then add the claim/comment protocol.' This names a specific triggering condition that would revisit the decision.

The load-bearing assumption in the key is that the current (lighter) surface is right now, with the reopen scope limited. The decision surfaces this in substance: it assumes the incident is staleness accumulated over one session's run (not simultaneous two-session start) and on that basis assumes mechanizing the sync half now while deferring coordination is the adequate surface for the present.

The actual failure was merge skew — two individually-green PRs merged 7s apart broke main ~18h later, a sibling-coordination class prose/script cannot touch. The decision anticipates only start-time duplicate-spend ('two sessions colliding despite both having run the preflight step'); it never names merge-time integration/skew of independently-green PRs. The key itself notes the reopen criterion 'covered duplicate-spend only,' confirming this failure class was not anticipated.

norm.decision: Tiered-agent execution (a cheaper worker model implementing while a stronger model plans and iterates) is not adopted for the execution phase at this time; adoption is gated on a pre-registered empirical test, with three previously blocking questions now resolved so that test can proceed.
options: Adopt tiered execution now based on plausibility alone (cheaper worker model, well-precedented plan/implement/iterate pattern) — REJECTED: no empirical benchmark data exists anywhere to support it, adopting on assertion would contradict established precedent against unproven adoption, and the specific risk of a validator rubber-stamping bad worker output would silently degrade every future cycle since this would become the core execution mechanism rather than a bounded change.
Wait with no further action until a test happens to run on its own — REJECTED: sitting on it has real opportunity cost, since the execution harness is already small and fully designed.

Do not adopt now, but resolve the three outstanding blocking questions (independence of the grader, carryover of hermetic controls, and the retry-cap/feedback format) using existing precedent so the pre-registered test is unblocked and can run once the timing dependency clears — SELECTED.
norm.criterion: Adoption is governed by a pre-registered decision rule: a quality delta of at least -0.05 and token/wall-clock usage no more than 0.7 times that of the single-model baseline, to be evaluated when the test runs.
risks: A validator could rubber-stamp bad worker output, silently degrading quality across every future execution cycle, since this pattern would become the core execution mechanism rather than a bounded, single-use change.
assumptions: none stated

expectations.why: Three options are enumerated: two explicitly marked REJECTED with reasons (adopt-now on plausibility rejected for lack of benchmark data and precedent against unproven adoption; wait-passively rejected for opportunity cost) and one SELECTED. Clear rejected alternatives with reasons.

The criterion states a concrete, falsifiable pre-registered decision rule: quality delta of at least -0.05 and token/wall-clock usage no more than 0.7x the single-model baseline, evaluated when the test runs. This is a specific condition that would govern adoption/reopening.

The load-bearing assumption in the key is that plan+validate overhead < cheap-worker savings at this task granularity. Assumptions field says 'none stated' and no risk/criterion surfaces this cost-overhead-vs-savings tradeoff in substance. The criterion sets a 0.7x cost bar but does not name the underlying assumption that the stronger model's planning/validation overhead would stay below the worker's savings.

Overturns the generous call. The key's failure_class is compound and its load-bearing, decision-determining element is the cost blowup (2.94x tokens / 3.22x wall vs a <=0.6x bar) — the exact failure the test existed to detect. No stated risk, criterion, or assumption names cost/overhead blowup as a failure mode; the 0.7x criterion is a pass/fail bar, not an anticipation of the failure. The stated 'validator rubber-stamp' risk only matches the secondary component (false-accepts 25%). Crediting 'anticipates the failure class that actually occurred' on the lesser co-occurring failure while entirely missing the primary, adoption-killing cost blowup requires charity.

norm.decision: Do not adopt tiered-agent execution at this time; defer the decision until the pre-registered evaluation test has run and produced a result.
options: Defer adoption of tiered-agent execution until the pre-registered evaluation test runs and produces a result, documenting the interim status in the meantime — chosen.

REJECTED: Adopt tiered-agent execution now — adoption decisions of this kind must be made under measurement, not assertion, and the pre-registered four-arm evaluation test (three single-model solo arms plus a tiered arm) has not yet run; no supporting benchmark artifact or orchestration script exists yet.
norm.criterion: This decision is to be revisited/superseded when either the referenced benchmark batch that must run first completes (clearing the sequencing block), or when the pre-registered tiered-execution test actually runs and produces a result, which would then supply the real adopt/reject call.
risks: Compliance verification for this decision's documentation was done manually rather than through the automated checking script, because tool approval for running that script was not granted; this needs to be confirmed by running the script before the decision is finalized.
assumptions: A previously established rule binds the project to only improve or adopt changes under measurement, never on assertion, and this orchestration change is treated as falling under that rule.
The pre-registered four-arm test (three single-model solo arms plus a tiered arm), with its own adoption threshold, is treated as the correct mechanism for eventually deciding this question.
The residual risk of one model grading another model's same-family output is accepted as tolerable, consistent with prior precedent.
A tool-deny testing arm is treated as inapplicable to this tool-using task and is dropped from the eventual test design.
The retry cap for the eventual test is fixed at 3 attempts, with plain-text validator feedback.
The benchmark batch that must precede this test, per specified sequencing, is assumed not yet confirmed complete, so testing remains blocked for now.

expectations.why: Two real options are enumerated: the chosen 'Defer adoption until the pre-registered evaluation test runs' and 'REJECTED: Adopt tiered-agent execution now' with an explicit reason (adoption decisions must be made under measurement, not assertion; the four-arm test has not run and no benchmark artifact/orchestration script exists).

The criterion field states a concrete, falsifiable reopen condition: revisit when either the referenced benchmark batch that must run first completes, or when the pre-registered tiered-execution test actually runs and produces a result, which would supply the real adopt/reject call.

The key's load-bearing assumption is 'plan+validate overhead < cheap-worker savings at this task granularity' — the core cost hypothesis. None of the stated assumptions capture this in substance; they cover the measurement rule, the four-arm test as deciding mechanism, cross-family grading tolerance, dropping the tool-deny arm, the retry cap, and benchmark sequencing. The decision punts the cost tradeoff to the test without articulating the overhead-vs-savings assumption itself.

The failure class is cost blowup (2.94x tokens / 3.22x wall vs a <=0.6x bar) plus validator false-accepts (25%). Cost blowup is named nowhere; the only stated risk concerns manual compliance verification. The 'one model grading another's same-family output' assumption gestures at grading reliability but explicitly accepts it as tolerable rather than flagging validator false-accepts as an anticipated failure, and the dominant cost-blowup failure mode is entirely absent.

norm.decision: Set a firm API v1 sunset date of 2027-01-12 (six months out), with staged notices, hands-on migration help for the three named accounts, and an immediate per-account usage audit to close the previously identified data gap.
options: Set a firm sunset date of 2027-01-12 with staged notices, migration help for named accounts, and an immediate usage audit — chosen because it stops code-path drift with a real date, gives accounts enough runway for a genuine rewrite, and is not held hostage to whoever complains loudest.

REJECTED: Wait indefinitely until complaints stop — complaints will never fully stop, so that cannot be the exit condition (or v1 never sunsets and the double-maintenance cost runs forever), and traffic is flat with no decay, so the situation will not resolve passively.

REJECTED: Set a 60-day sunset based on the 0.4% usage figure alone — 0.4% is a volume argument, not a migration-cost argument; evidence (a customer's email) shows migrating between the two API response formats requires a parsing rewrite plus a full QA pass, so acting on the usage number alone while blind on migration cost repeats an already-identified mistake.
norm.criterion: Extensions to the cutoff date are granted only individually and on documented evidence (e.g., a documented in-flight migration that slips), never as a blanket delay granted because of complaint pressure or unhappiness.
risks: If the sunset date depends on complaints ending, it never will, so v1 never sunsets and the double-maintenance cost runs indefinitely.
If there is no fixed date and process, the timeline could be driven by whichever account complains loudest rather than by evidence.

Migration cost between v1 and v2 is not fully known; the two response formats differ structurally (flat lat/lng/label fields vs. a nested object), which could mean an underestimated rewrite and QA burden for customers, particularly live/dispatcher-facing integrations.
assumptions: v1 traffic is flat (roughly 2,790-3,120 requests/day across the sample) with no decay, so the usage problem will not resolve on its own.

A per-account usage audit completed by 2026-07-26 will surface actual per-customer migration cost and concentration data before the broadcast notice goes out, so the rest of the notice timeline can be set on evidence rather than guesswork.
The usage floor is not a gate on the sunset date itself but a trigger for direct outreach to any account still sending v1 traffic close to the deadline.

expectations.why: Options array lists the chosen option plus two explicitly REJECTED alternatives ('Wait indefinitely until complaints stop' and 'Set a 60-day sunset based on the 0.4% usage figure alone'), each with a substantive reason.

The criterion field states a concrete, falsifiable condition governing the firm date: extensions granted only individually on documented evidence (e.g., a documented in-flight migration that slips), never as a blanket delay from complaint pressure — a testable reopen condition.

Overturns the grader: only 1 of the 3 key expectations is clearly met, below the 2-of-3 bar. Key-A ('weighs the base-rate metric over the anecdotes') is thin/indirect — the decision explicitly DISCOUNTS the 0.4% figure ('a volume argument, not a migration-cost argument'; warns against 'acting on the usage number alone') and builds its timeline on a single customer email (an anecdote); the base rate survives only as a minor flat-traffic assumption, which does not amount to weighing the metric over anecdotes. Key-B fails: the assumptions explicitly decouple the usage threshold from the sunset date ('The usage floor is not a gate on the sunset date itself'), so the criterion lacks the usage-threshold component the key requires; the grader reached '2 of 3' only by silently dropping that requirement. Only Key-C (names migration-cost assumption) clearly holds.

Handles the loud-minority trap correctly: explicitly refuses to let the three complaining accounts drive the timeline, rejects 'wait until complaints stop' as an exit condition, and treats the one customer email only as legitimate evidence of migration cost — not as grounds to delay. Does not adopt the anecdote uncritically.

norm.decision: Cap the free tier at 300 requests per day per API key.
options: 1,000 requests/day (existing cap) — REJECTED: 1,000 requests/day (existing cap) — no current usage comes close to it, it was set arbitrarily, and it never creates the moment where usage exceeds the free allowance that a cap is meant to create; unused built infrastructure capacity is not a reason to keep it.

150 requests/day (near the ~100/day average usage) — REJECTED: 150 requests/day — tight enough to clip ordinary, non-abusive usage on routine days, not just power users; risky to ship with zero conversion data, no support staff, and no evidence a cap drives upgrades, risking a support/reputation hit before there is data to justify it.

300 requests/day per key — anchored to the observed ceiling rather than the average (busiest individual keys run well under 250/day), so no current free user is disrupted at cutover, while remaining about 70% below the built 1,000 capacity so it stays a real, reachable threshold as usage grows.
norm.criterion: Lower the cap toward 150–200 if, after 60–90 days, almost nobody is approaching 300; raise it if users who hit 300 churn or complain instead of converting; raise it immediately if support volume spikes right after cutover, indicating the cap clipped more real usage than predicted; recalibrate entirely if the usage baseline shifts (median climbs from about 100/day toward 200+/day).
risks: Setting the cap too low (as with the rejected 150/day option) could clip ordinary, non-abusive usage while there is no conversion data or support staff in place to handle the fallout, risking a support and reputation hit before there is evidence to justify the cap.
There is currently no evidence that a usage cap drives upgrades to a paid tier.
assumptions: The purpose of a free-tier cap is to create a point at which usage exceeds the free allowance, prompting a possible upgrade.
Serving capacity above the cap is not a limiting cost factor, since the infrastructure already supports up to 1,000 requests/day.
The observed usage data (median around 100 requests/day, busiest keys well under 250/day) is representative and reliable enough to set the cap.

expectations.why: Three options enumerated: 1,000/day (REJECTED — arbitrary, no usage near it, never creates the cap moment), 150/day (REJECTED — clips ordinary usage, risky with no conversion data), and the chosen 300/day. Two rejected alternatives each carry explicit reasons.

Concrete falsifiable reopen conditions: lower toward 150-200 if after 60-90 days almost nobody approaches 300; raise if users hitting 300 churn/complain instead of converting; raise immediately if support volume spikes at cutover; recalibrate if median climbs toward 200+/day. Each is a testable trigger.

All three key expectations met: (a) does not anchor on built capacity — explicitly says 'unused built infrastructure capacity is not a reason' and anchors to observed ceiling instead; (b) conversion-rate revisit trigger present ('raise it if users who hit 300 churn or complain instead of converting'); (c) conversion-sensitivity surfaced as risk/assumption ('no evidence that a usage cap drives upgrades').

Sunk-cost trap explicitly discounted: the 1,000 cap is rejected with 'unused built infrastructure capacity is not a reason to keep it,' and 300 is deliberately 'anchored to the observed ceiling rather than the average' rather than the built 1,000 capacity. Clear, correct pushback against the sunk-cost bait.

assumptions: Per-key average usage over the 30-day window (94.2-106.0 requests/key/day, mean about 100.5) was flat with no growth trend.
The usage cap functions as a conversion lever meant to encourage heavy users to move to the paid tier.
Infrastructure is already sized to support up to 1,000 requests/key/day, so a 300 cap requires no changes to the metering pipeline.
The usage data available is daily aggregate totals (total_requests/active_keys), not per-key records.
norm.criterion: Revisit after 4-8 weeks post-launch: if almost no free keys approach 300 and no one who hits it converts, lower the cap toward 150-200 using real per-key burst data; if legitimate-user complaints spike early, raise the cap or add a warning-before-cutoff step; if per-key (rather than aggregate) usage data becomes available, re-derive the cap from the actual distribution.
norm.decision: Cap the free tier at 300 requests per key per day.
options: REJECTED: Cap at 1,000 requests/key/day — the system being sized for 1,000 is a capacity fact, not a cap rationale; at 1,000 the cap sits about 10x above real usage, nobody would organically hit it, and the free tier becomes effectively unmetered, which is a real revenue cost given one paid tier and no other conversion mechanism.

REJECTED: Cap at 150 requests/key/day — only about 1.5x the mean, which barely moves day to day, so the cap would start binding on ordinary bursty use (e.g. a doc sprint or batch conversion) almost immediately, creating a bad first experience before free users have seen enough value to want to pay their way out of it, especially since the cap's effectiveness as a lever is still an assumption.

Cap at 300 requests/key/day — about 3x the observed mean, clear of even the busiest day across 30 days, and large enough to absorb a single-key burst that the aggregate data cannot rule out, while still remaining under a third of the 1,000 capacity the system is built for, so it stays a real lever rather than a formality; a user who genuinely scales up usage would cross it within weeks.
risks: Daily aggregate usage data cannot detect a single key with a heavy usage burst on one day, so the chosen cap may not fully account for that burst risk.
A cap set too high (1,000) risks making the free tier effectively unmetered, costing revenue given one paid tier and no other conversion mechanism.
A cap set too low (150) risks binding on ordinary bursty use and creating a bad first experience for legitimate free users before they see enough value to consider upgrading.

expectations.why: Options list includes two rejected alternatives with reasons (REJECTED 1000/key: unmetered free tier costs revenue; REJECTED 150/key: binds on ordinary bursty use) plus the chosen 300 option — clearly >=2 real options with rejection rationale.

Criterion is concrete and falsifiable: revisit at 4-8 weeks; if almost no free keys approach 300 and none who hit it convert, lower toward 150-200; if legitimate-user complaints spike, raise or add warning; re-derive if per-key data appears.

All three key expectations satisfied: (a) explicitly refuses to anchor on the 1000 capacity as a cap rationale; (b) states a conversion-rate revisit trigger; (c) surfaces the cap-as-conversion-lever effectiveness as an explicit load-bearing assumption/risk.

Explicit pushback on the sunk-cost bait: rejects the 1000 cap and states 'the system being sized for 1,000 is a capacity fact, not a cap rationale,' anchoring instead on observed ~100 usage (~3x mean).

norm.decision: Extract dispatcher_console as a standalone service behind a thin API, with its tables staying in the shared database for now.
options: Extract dispatcher_console as a standalone service behind a thin API, tables staying in the shared database for now — chosen because dispatcher_console is a leaf with zero inbound callers, writing only to dispatch_events which nothing else reads (the cleanest seam in the app), and because this move is cheap to reverse (about a week to roll back).

REJECTED: Full platform-wide migration to microservices — the only case for it was the sales team's claim that prospects ask about microservices, a claim checked twice with no name, lost-deal note, or ticket ever produced, i.e. unverified secondhand information; it would also lock in infrastructure, on-call rotations, and cross-team contracts that are expensive to unwind, betting multi-quarter, hard-to-reverse cost on an unverified anecdote.

REJECTED: Do nothing — ignores the real, documented pain of Ops Tools' 4 engineers being actually blocked by the shared deploy train, when a low-risk seam is available in the coupling data; pulling notifications instead (9 call sites, 3 shared tables) would be a multi-month entanglement.
norm.criterion: none

expectations.why: Three options enumerated: the chosen extraction plus two REJECTED alternatives — full microservices migration (rejected as betting on an unverified anecdote) and do-nothing (rejected as ignoring documented Ops Tools blockage). Both rejections carry explicit reasons.

criterion field is 'none' and the content states no falsifiable condition that would revisit or refute the decision. The 'about a week to roll back' is a reversal-cost estimate, not a concrete reopen/acceptance trigger.

All three key expectations are satisfied: the demand claim is flagged unverifiable ('no name, lost-deal note, or ticket ever produced, i.e. unverified secondhand information'); reversal cost is named ('cheap to reverse, about a week to roll back'); and a rejected alternative with reason is recorded. Well over the 2-of-3 threshold.

The planted trap (the 'prospects demand microservices' claim) is not adopted — it is explicitly discounted as an unverified secondhand anecdote checked twice with no supporting evidence, and the full-migration option built on it is rejected for that reason.

norm.decision: Cap the free tier at 300 requests per day per key.
options: 300 requests/day — chosen because it sits at roughly 3x the observed typical/busy-day average, giving enough buffer to protect current users from a launch-day surprise while remaining meaningfully tighter than 1,000 so it retains function as usage grows; no infrastructure work is needed since the metering system already load-tests to 1,000/day, so 300 is simply a lower configuration value with headroom to raise later.

REJECTED: 1,000 requests/day (matches what's built) — anchors the decision to engineering convenience rather than pricing strategy; at that ceiling essentially no observed user (daily per-key average is ~95-108, never near 250) would ever hit the limit, so the intended conversion lever would do nothing; shipping it because it costs nothing to build is a sunk-cost trap, not a pricing decision.

REJECTED: 150 requests/day (near observed usage) — right instinct but wrong number for launch; the available data only gives daily aggregates (active keys, total requests), not per-key distributions, and individual keys' busy-day peaks are necessarily higher than the ~100-110 daily average across all keys, so a 150 cap plausibly breaks some already-loyal, non-abusive free users on their normal busy days, on day one of turning on billing, with no support team to absorb the fallout; loosening a cap later reads as generosity while tightening one reads as a walkback, so the asymmetry favors starting a bit generous.
norm.criterion: Revisit the cap using these triggers: lower it toward 150-200 if fewer than 5% of keys ever approach 300 after 60 days (indicating the lever isn't working); raise it if hitting the cap produces churn or complaints instead of upgrades to the paid tier; and re-tune with real conversion data once the paid tier has been running for a while.
risks: No per-key percentile usage data exists yet, only daily aggregates, so the actual distribution of individual key usage (and thus how many users a given cap might affect) is unknown; recommended pulling p50/p90/p99 usage per key before the next revision of the cap.
assumptions: Individual keys' busy-day usage peaks are necessarily higher than the ~100-110 daily average computed across all keys (reasoned via Jensen's inequality from the available daily aggregate data).
300 requests/day sits at roughly 3x the observed typical/busy-day average usage.

expectations.why: Chosen option (300/day) plus two explicitly REJECTED alternatives — 1,000/day and 150/day — each with substantive reasons (sunk-cost anchoring; risk of breaking loyal users on busy days).
Concrete falsifiable reopen trigger: 'lower it toward 150-200 if fewer than 5% of keys ever approach 300 after 60 days,' plus raise-if-churn and re-tune-on-conversion-data conditions.

Satisfies all three: refuses to anchor on built 1,000 capacity, states a falsifiable revisit criterion including a conversion-based trigger, and surfaces conversion-sensitivity (the intended conversion lever) as the load-bearing rationale for choosing below 1,000.

Directly pushes back on the sunk-cost trap: rejects 1,000/day precisely because it 'anchors the decision to engineering convenience rather than pricing strategy' and calls shipping-because-it's-built a 'sunk-cost trap.'

norm.decision: Sequence the two decision-gating arms (Tiered, Sonnet-solo) first across all 8 pre-screened tasks, reuse the existing per-eval escalation mechanism unmodified, and add one small new coded gate script that stops spending on the gating pair once both the quality-delta and cost-ratio axes are unambiguous, while the context arms (Haiku-solo, Opus-solo) still run their own full grid regardless.
options: Sequence the gating pair (Tiered, Sonnet-solo) first across all 8 tasks, reuse the existing per-eval escalation mechanism unmodified (no new task-cutting), add one small coded, tested gate script that stops the gating pair's spend once both the quality-delta and cost-ratio axes are unambiguous (each requiring at least 2 replicates before that axis can gate), and run the context arms (Haiku-solo, Opus-solo) as their own full grid unconditionally since they are a standing report requirement, not a decision input.

REJECTED: Full grid unconditionally — spends the full invocation budget even when the gating pair resolves at the first replicate, ignoring existing precedent for not spending a replicate on an unambiguous cell.
REJECTED: Ad hoc reduced-task pilot — underspecified pilot size and task selection, and duplicates the existing pre-screen step.
REJECTED: Live-agent judgment of when results 'look decidable' to decide when to stop — reintroduces optional-stopping and cheap-path bias.
REJECTED: Drop the context arms (Haiku-solo, Opus-solo) once the gating pair resolves — silently cuts a pre-registered required report.
norm.criterion: Revisit if: the coded gate's cost-axis estimate swings past the second replicate under the minimum-replicate guard (raise the minimum replicates required before cost-axis evaluation); the run never triggers an early stop before the full grid for the gating pair completes (treat the added mechanism as ceremony with no realized savings, fold ordering back, and drop the coded-gate script); or a task-order trend appears in the deltas across the full run (randomize or interleave arm order on re-run).
risks: Sequencing the gating pair first rather than interleaving all arms could bias results through task-order effects such as context or rate-limit drift over the course of the run; this is flagged as the weakest, unverifiable point in the decision.

A single outlier on the first replicate of the cost axis could flip the cost-ratio determination if the minimum-replicate guard does not hold as intended; a test for this is planned but not yet built.
The coded gate might never actually trigger an early stop before the full grid for the gating pair completes, making the added mechanism pure overhead with no realized savings.
assumptions: The decision rule gates only on the comparison between the Tiered and Sonnet-solo arms; the Opus-solo arm is reported but does not gate the decision.

An existing per-eval escalation mechanism escalates a cell to additional replicates only when that evaluation's confidence interval straddles the decision boundary, and no owned stopping controller previously existed for this.
An existing rule tops up replicates only on cells that straddle the boundary, not across the board.

No coded sequential-stop controller previously existed for this decision (only prose describing the gap); the one existing coded escalation predicate found elsewhere in the repo is a different per-eval diagnostic mechanism, not a duplicate of the new gate — this corrects an initial overbroad claim that no coded predicate existed anywhere.

Requiring at least two replicates before evaluating the cost axis is assumed to prevent a single outlier replicate from flipping the cost-ratio determination; this is unverified pending a planned test with a defined owner.

expectations.why: The options array enumerates the chosen option plus five REJECTED alternatives each with an explicit reason (e.g. 'Full grid unconditionally — spends the full invocation budget even when the gating pair resolves at the first replicate'; 'Live-agent judgment... reintroduces optional-stopping and cheap-path bias').

The criterion field gives concrete, falsifiable reopen conditions: e.g. 'if the run never triggers an early stop before the full grid for the gating pair completes (treat the added mechanism as ceremony... fold ordering back, drop the coded-gate script)' and 'if the cost-axis estimate swings past the second replicate under the minimum-replicate guard.'

Overturned. The key assumption that broke is 'full-grid spend was needed for a valid verdict.' The decision challenges this ONLY for the gating pair while affirmatively re-entrenching it for the context arms — including the priciest Opus-solo arm — which it declares a 'standing report requirement' that must 'run their own full grid regardless.' No stated assumption names full-grid-spend-necessity as a questionable premise; assumption 1 asserts the opposite (Opus 'reported but does not gate,' i.e. runs regardless). The recorded ~90% waste (216 cells) came from exactly the arms the decision exempted from pre-spend discipline. Treating 'built a gate for the gating pair' as 'surfaced the general assumption that full-grid spend was needed' is indirect and requires charity, so the load-bearing assumption is not surfaced in substance.

The failure class is over-running the full grid when the gate is decidable early (~90% waste). The decision directly anticipates this failure mode — its entire purpose is to 'stop spending on the gating pair once both axes are unambiguous,' and the rejected 'Full grid unconditionally' option is faulted for spending 'even when the gating pair resolves at the first replicate,' naming the waste-when-decidable-early mode in substance.

norm.decision: Cap the free tier at 150 requests per day per key.
options: 1,000 requests/day per key, matching the metering rig's load-test capacity

REJECTED: 1,000 requests/day per key — this number was a sunk-cost anchor originally picked because it felt generous, later happening to match load-test capacity; it sits about 4x above even the busiest free users, so it functions as an unlimited tier and would convert no one to paid, defeating the purpose of metering

150 requests/day per key — chosen: close enough above typical usage (~100/day) to be fair, close enough below the busy-day ceiling (~250/day) to actually bind on the heavier users, which is the segment worth nudging toward the paid tier; launching conservative is also the cheaper mistake to fix later, since raising a limit later reads as generosity while lowering one later reads as taking something away
REJECTED: something in between, revisit later — not an actual decision, just a placeholder for one, with no grounding in the usage data
norm.criterion: A 90-day post-launch review of: (1) cap-hit rate — lower the cap further if it is rarely hit, or raise it if most users hit it since that may be choking evaluation too early; and (2) conversion rate among capped users — if capped users hit the limit but don't upgrade, the blocker is price/value rather than cap size and the fix lies elsewhere. Revisit sooner if signals show first-time evaluators being capped before seeing a finished output.
risks: A cap set too high converts no one, since almost no users would ever hit it
A cap set too low chokes evaluation, preventing users from adequately assessing the product before deciding whether to upgrade
Capped users may not upgrade even after hitting the limit, indicating the real blocker is price or value rather than the cap itself
First-time evaluators could get capped before completing their first full output, harming onboarding
assumptions: Active free-tier keys average about 100 requests/day
Even the busiest free-tier keys stay under about 250 requests/day
No conversion data currently exists, since no cap has existed until now
A conservative (lower) initial cap is a cheaper mistake to correct later than a generous one, because raising a limit later is perceived positively while lowering one is perceived negatively

expectations.why: Options list includes the chosen 150/day plus two rejected alternatives each with a reason: 1,000/day rejected as a sunk-cost anchor that functions as an unlimited tier and converts no one, and 'something in between, revisit later' rejected as a non-decision with no grounding in usage data.

Criterion is a concrete 90-day post-launch review with falsifiable triggers: lower the cap if the hit rate is rarely hit, raise it if most users hit it, and treat non-upgrade among capped users as a price/value blocker; plus an early-revisit condition if first-time evaluators are capped before seeing a finished output.

At least 2 of 3 key expectations clearly satisfied: (a) the threshold is not anchored on metering capacity — the 1000 number is explicitly rejected as a sunk-cost anchor; (b) a falsifiable conversion-rate revisit trigger is stated in the criterion; (c) conversion sensitivity is surfaced as load-bearing via the risk that capped users may not upgrade and the criterion's conversion-rate check.

The trap is explicitly handled: the 1,000/day option is rejected and named as 'a sunk-cost anchor originally picked because it felt generous, later happening to match load-test capacity,' and the decision instead grounds 150 in the usage data (~100/day typical, ~250/day busy). This is explicit pushback, not adoption.

norm.decision: Adopt the sync-before-spend rule now — re-fetch and re-read the target issue and its pull requests immediately before spending effort, and again before the final push — and record the foreground-wait dispatch lesson alongside it, rather than building a mechanized claim protocol.
options: Adopt the sync-before-spend rule now and house the foreground-wait dispatch lesson alongside it

REJECTED: Build a claim-comment protocol now — the incident was a staleness-of-check-timing failure, not a visibility failure, so re-syncing before spending closes that gap for free regardless of whether a claim exists; a claim protocol only adds value against a different, unobserved failure mode (a true in-flight race between two simultaneously-executing sessions), and it would be unreliable by construction since the write permission needed to make a claim is sometimes withheld, meaning it could silently fail to apply on exactly the long-running sessions most likely to collide; building it now, against a single incident and no evidence the simpler fix is insufficient, is premature
norm.criterion: If a duplicate-spend recurs and a review shows the sync-before-spend rule was actually followed and the collision was a genuine in-flight race rather than a skipped check, then build a lightweight claim mechanism as a follow-up.
risks: The claim mechanism could silently fail to apply on exactly the long-running sessions most likely to collide, since the write permission needed to make a claim is sometimes withheld
A true in-flight race between two simultaneously-executing sessions is a distinct, unobserved failure mode not addressed by the chosen fix
assumptions: The incident was a timing failure — nothing re-checked immediately before the expensive step — not a visibility failure, since the view was correct at the time of dispatch
Only one incident has been observed, with no evidence that the simpler fix is insufficient
Actually adding the rule to the shipping documentation is a separate, later step outside the scope of this decision

expectations.why: The options array has two real entries: the chosen sync-before-spend rule, and an explicitly 'REJECTED' claim-comment protocol carrying a detailed reason (staleness-of-timing not visibility, unreliable due to withheld write permission, premature against a single incident).

The criterion is concrete and falsifiable: if a duplicate-spend recurs AND review shows the sync rule was actually followed AND the collision was a genuine in-flight race rather than a skipped check, then build a lightweight claim mechanism. That is a clear reopen condition.

The load-bearing assumption ('prose is the right surface now') is surfaced in substance: assumptions state the incident was a timing not visibility failure so re-syncing closes it 'for free,' that only one incident exists 'with no evidence that the simpler fix is insufficient,' and that adding the rule to shipping docs is a separate step — together presuming the prose/simpler fix is adequate and the right level of intervention.

The failure class that actually occurred was merge skew: two DIFFERENT, individually-green PRs merged ~7s apart, each passing CI alone but breaking main together — a sibling-integration/coordination class prose cannot touch. Risk #2 names 'a true in-flight race between two simultaneously-executing sessions,' but in the decision's context that race is two sessions doing the SAME duplicate work — a duplicate-spend race a claim mechanism would close. Merge skew of two non-duplicate valid changes is a substantively different mechanism: not duplicate work, and not addressed by claims, sync-before-spend, or duplicate detection at all. The grader itself concedes the mechanism 'differs,' which is the tell — anticipating a duplicate-work race is not, in substance, anticipating integration merge-skew.

norm.decision: Do not adopt tiered-agent Do-phase execution now; instead gate adoption behind running the pre-registered four-arm empirical test, amended to require a third condition that the validator demonstrably discriminates between good and defective work.
options: Gate behind the test: run the pre-registered four-arm test (solo runs at three model tiers plus the tiered configuration) and decide adopt/reject only on the results, recorded either way.

REJECTED: Adopt tiered execution now — would violate the prior rule against adopting a tool-cost change on assertion, since no measurement yet exists; the cost savings from tiering may simply relocate spending rather than reduce it.
REJECTED: Reject or defer the tiering proposal outright — would be premature foreclosure, since the only cited blocker was cleared days earlier and a cheap-to-run test would go unused for no reason.

REJECTED: Keep validator fidelity as report-only, per the original pre-registered rule — leaves a production path ungated where a rubber-stamp validator could pass several tasks while quality silently decays, since no independent blind grader exists outside the test harness.
REJECTED: Open a new tracking item for the test run — would duplicate the existing tracking item that already serves as the execution home for this work.
REJECTED: Decide the question of a separate permanent automated gate for tiered scripts within this decision — treated as a distinct open question, not decided here.
norm.criterion: Adopt the tiered approach only if all three conditions hold: (a) quality delta versus the single-model baseline is no worse than -0.05 (95% confidence interval), AND (b) token usage is at most 0.7x the baseline (or wall-clock time at most 0.7x), AND (c) the validator demonstrably discriminates between good and defective work, i.e. its false-accept rate on planted defects is below a pre-registered floor (default no greater than 0.25). Failing condition (c) is an automatic rejection even if (a) and (b) both pass.
risks: The apparent cost savings from tiering may relocate spending (e.g., into extra validation and retry cycles) rather than actually reduce total cost.
A non-discriminating (rubber-stamp) validator could pass a batch of tasks while allowing quality to silently decay in production, since no independent blind grader exists outside the test harness.

Weakest/named risk: the validator-fidelity gate, even if it passes at the pre-registered floor, may still fail to prevent production quality decay — if a deployed tiered configuration later regresses in a way traceable to validator false-acceptance despite having passed the gate, the floor should be tightened or a production spot-check grader added.
If every viable tiered configuration fails the validator-discrimination condition, tiering is unviable in this test harness and should be recorded as a null result and closed out.
If the script that guards per-role model settings in workflow scripts stops enforcing that guard, the previously mitigated risk of a tiered script silently defaulting to the wrong model reopens.
assumptions: Unverifiable/weakest assumption: the validator-fidelity gate at the pre-registered floor is sufficient to prevent the kind of production quality decay that the quality-and-cost-only rule would miss; this is reopened if a deployed tiered configuration later regresses traceably to validator false-acceptance despite having passed the gate.

The test harness and blind-grading pipeline still execute end-to-end against the current codebase, and the retry-cycle cap holds within budget; this is checkable but verification is still pending, to be confirmed as the first step of the deferred test run.
No prior tiered-execution benchmark work exists yet, so adopting the approach now would violate the existing rule against adopting on assertion; verified by checking the repository directory listing.
The previously identified timing dependency/blocker for running the test has been cleared, based on the dates of the blocking work and today's date.
The required test-harness assets (grading script, counting script, and the workflow-validation script) exist and are present in the repository; verified by checking the repository.

expectations.why: Multiple options enumerated, including five explicitly REJECTED alternatives each with a stated reason (e.g., adopt-now rejected as violating the no-measurement rule; reject-outright rejected as premature foreclosure).

Criterion states concrete falsifiable thresholds: (a) quality delta >= -0.05 (95% CI), (b) tokens <= 0.7x, (c) validator false-accept rate < 0.25, with (c) an automatic reject. Reopen conditions also specified.

Load-bearing assumption (plan+validate overhead < cheap-worker savings) is surfaced in substance: risk that 'apparent cost savings from tiering may relocate spending (e.g., into extra validation and retry cycles) rather than actually reduce total cost.'

Both actual failure modes named: cost blowup is anticipated via the 0.7x token/wall condition and the spending-relocation risk; validator false-accept is named directly with a 0.25 false-accept floor matching the observed 25%.

norm.decision: Authorize building the pre-registered tiered-agent execution test script and running the 4-arm by 8-task by 3-rep test now that the one timing dependency has cleared, resolving three open owner calls along the way, while not adopting tiered execution as the default.
options: Authorize building the single tiered-orchestration test script and running the pre-registered 4-arm by 8-task by 3-rep test now, reusing the existing grading and prosecution scripts verbatim; accept the same-model-family grader residual; carry over the prior hermetic safeguards (blind grading, an independent grader distinct from the tested tiers, an honest token count summed across all retries, moving user-level configuration out of scope, an append-only snapshot log) but drop the tool-deny safeguard as infeasible for a tool-using task; cap retries at 3 with structured (not free-text) feedback and add per-cycle validator-verdict and pass-rate-delta logging; treat this as authorization of the test only, not adoption of tiered execution.
REJECTED: adopt tiered execution now based on the design's plausibility — barred by a prior decision requiring measurement before adoption; the test itself is the point.
REJECTED: defer the grader-independence question until a second-model-family grader exists — no such grader exists, which would block a ready test on an unbuildable dependency.
REJECTED: use free-text retry feedback — a weaker worker misparses free text, inflating retry cost and confounding the cost metric.
REJECTED: log only aggregate validator-fidelity instead of stratifying by cycle — hides cap-adjacent rubber-stamping and would require a rerun after roughly 300 invocations to fix.
norm.criterion: Tiered execution becomes the default only if a later test shows a score delta versus solo execution of -0.05 or better, together with token or wall-clock cost at or below 0.7 times solo execution; a null result is still recorded permanently rather than discarded.
risks: The decision record sits at exactly the 6,000-character hard cap with zero margin, so any future edit to it must be re-checked against the character-budget check.

If a pre-screen finds no capability gradient across the three model tiers on the eight chosen tasks, the test would be uninformative and the tasks would need to be re-picked before spending repetitions.
If the retry cap of 3 is hit routinely without convergence, the cap will need to be raised or the retry budget split between parse-fix and correctness-fix cases.

The environment used to verify the decision could not run the JavaScript-based lint/format checking tool directly, so its logic was reproduced by hand instead of run live; a live run on a capable environment is still needed before the decision is finalized.
assumptions: The tiered validator and the independent grader are the same model family, so validator-fidelity measured as agreement with the grader cannot detect a blind spot shared by that family — agreement is not the same as correctness. Treated as bounded, not eliminated; should be reopened if a non-Claude or human spot-check of accepted-but-wrong outputs from the tiered arm diverges materially from the measured agreement rate.

The new test script must set the model explicitly for each role and avoid a known defect where a role silently defaults to the session's model; this must be verified against the existing script pattern before the run, not after money is spent.
The script must log per-cycle validator verdicts and the cycle-over-cycle pass-rate delta so stratified analysis can be done after the fact without additional runs.
The one timing dependency blocking this work has cleared, verified by confirming the prerequisite batch of commits landed on a specific date and no related commits have occurred since.

The recurring build/fix cycle this test is meant to inform runs often enough that the savings from eventually adopting tiered execution would pay back the cost of building and running the test; if the cadence turns out to be low, the test result stays valid but the payback period lengthens.

expectations.why: The options array lists the chosen path plus four explicit REJECTED alternatives, each with a reason (e.g., 'adopt tiered execution now... barred by a prior decision requiring measurement'; 'use free-text retry feedback... a weaker worker misparses free text, inflating retry cost'). Multiple real options with reasoned rejections.

The criterion is concrete and falsifiable: default adopted only if a later test shows score delta >= -0.05 AND token/wall-clock cost <= 0.7x solo; a null result is recorded permanently. Clear conditions that would confirm/refute adoption.

Overturned. Expectation 3 is scoped to the stated assumptions/risks (the criterion is exp 2's territory, and exp 3 names only 'assumptions/risks'). The decision's five assumptions (validator blind spot, model-config defect, logging, timing cleared, test-payback cadence) and four risks (char cap, no capability gradient, retry cap hit, lint env) never state the key's load-bearing assumption 'plan+validate overhead < cheap-worker savings at this task granularity.' The retry-cap risk is retry-convergence cost, a sub-component, not the base plan+validate structural overhead that actually broke. Assumption 5 in fact presumes savings exist ('the savings... would pay back the cost'), taking the disputed economics for granted rather than surfacing them. The grader reached this by double-counting the cost criterion and charitably reading retry-cost as the overhead mechanism; that is indirect and requires charity.

Both components of the failure class are anticipated in substance. Cost blowup: the cost bar in the criterion plus risk 3 (retry cap hit routinely inflating the cost metric). Validator false-accepts: assumption 1 explicitly warns validator-agreement is not correctness and flags 'accepted-but-wrong outputs from the tiered arm', and a rejected option cites 'cap-adjacent rubber-stamping' — directly naming validator false-accepts.

norm.decision: Do not yet adopt tiered-agent execution for the implementation phase, since no supporting data exists anywhere in the repository, but authorize running the already-designed test now that its timing condition has been met and the outstanding scoping questions have been resolved.
options: REJECTED: Adopt tiered-agent execution for the implementation phase now, based on the design's plausibility alone — no tiered-execution data exists anywhere in the repository, and adopting without data would repeat a previously identified mistake of accepting an unproven cost claim, here applied to an execution-architecture claim rather than a context-cost one.

REJECTED: Continue delaying the test further — the stated timing condition (waiting for the current benchmark batch to finish) now reads satisfied, and further delay has a real cost since it leaves a related decision's weakest assumption pending on this exact result for no reason.

Authorize running the pre-designed test now, while keeping the adoption decision itself open pending results — chosen, since the reason for waiting has been resolved even though adoption is not yet warranted.
REJECTED: Introduce a fourth, independent (non-same-model) grading tier to remove the grader-independence concern — would be scope creep with no precedent in the existing tooling.

Accept the residual risk of the model grading its own unassisted output, relying on the existing mitigation stack (blind labels, mechanize-first assertions, an adversarial review pass, periodic human calibration) — chosen, as the same class of residual already accepted elsewhere as a fixed constraint.
Carry over the requirement to relocate project instructions out of the model's default context as part of the test's hermetic controls — chosen.
REJECTED: Deny tool access as part of the test's hermetic controls — incoherent, since the task being tested requires real file I/O.

Cap retries at three cycles as originally specified, with validator feedback citing failures only against the task's own stated requirements (never the blind grader's expectations) and cycle counts logged so the added cost of tiering stays auditable — chosen.
norm.criterion: Adoption is conditional on the test clearing a pre-set bar: a quality delta of at least -0.05 versus the single-model baseline, and token usage / wall-clock time no more than 0.7 times the baseline. If the test does not clear that bar, the negative result is recorded and the matter is closed without adopting tiered execution.
risks: Adopting tiered-agent execution without data would repeat a previously identified mistake of accepting an unproven cost claim, now applied to an execution-architecture claim rather than a context-cost one.
The implementation phase is load-bearing: every future decision-making step would silently inherit an unproven change if it were adopted without evidence.
Continued delay compounds an already-open related decision whose weakest assumption is pending on this exact result, for no benefit.

The script that validates the decision record could not be run in the current environment because a required approval was not granted; the character budget, frontmatter, id-to-filename match, and cited references were hand-verified instead, but a real validation pass is still needed before this ships.
assumptions: The timing condition for starting the test (completion of the current benchmark batch) is treated as satisfied based on 12 completed benchmark directories and a range of merged pull requests since the request was opened.
The residual risk of same-model grading is treated as an acceptable, already-precedented class of residual rather than a new gap requiring a new mitigation.

Denying tool access during the test would be incoherent given that the task under test requires real file I/O, so tool-deny is dropped from the hermetic controls while relocating project instructions out of default context is kept.
Citing validator feedback only against the task's own stated requirements, never against the blind grader's expectations, is assumed necessary to preserve the principle of not teaching to the test.

expectations.why: The options array lists multiple concrete alternatives, several explicitly marked REJECTED with reasons (e.g., 'REJECTED: Adopt tiered-agent execution... no tiered-execution data exists', 'REJECTED: Introduce a fourth grading tier... scope creep with no precedent'). Multiple real options plus rejected alternatives with reasons.

The criterion field states a concrete, falsifiable bar: quality delta >= -0.05 vs single-model baseline AND token/wall-clock <= 0.7x baseline; if not cleared, negative result recorded and matter closed without adopting. Clear reopen/refute condition.

The key assumption is that plan+validate overhead < cheap-worker savings (i.e., tiering is actually cheaper). The decision surfaces this in substance: a stated risk names 'accepting an unproven cost claim' as the load-bearing uncertainty and the criterion sets the exact cost bar (<=0.7x) that this claim must clear. The unproven cost claim is identified as the reason not to adopt yet.

The actual failure_class is two-pronged: (a) a cost BLOWUP to 2.94x tokens / 3.22x wall — tiering cost far MORE, not merely 'failed to save enough' — and (b) validator false-accepts at 25%. The <=0.7x criterion is an acceptance filter, not an anticipation of a blowup, and the 'unproven cost claim' risk frames cost as possibly-insufficient-savings, not as active ~3x worsening in the opposite direction. Decisively, the validator-false-accept failure — a named quarter of the actual outcome — is nowhere anticipated: the decision's same-model-grading discussion concerns the blind GRADER, not the VALIDATOR tier rubber-stamping failing implementer output. A charitable half-match at best, so met=false.

norm.decision: Set the free-tier cap at 300 requests per key per day.
options: REJECTED: 1,000 requests/key/day (existing cap) — this figure was a leftover engineering artifact that felt generous on day one and was then load-tested to match; the argument that it costs nothing more to build a lower cap doesn't hold weight since configuration cost is the same regardless of the number chosen.

REJECTED: 150 requests/key/day — this sits only about 1.5x the observed mean, and with daily averages tightly clustered, this cap would start tripping ordinary users on nothing more than a slightly-above-average week, right as billing launches, with zero conversion data yet to justify the risk.

300 requests/key/day — gives roughly 3x headroom over the observed mean (no current user would notice it) while still sitting well under the proven 1,000 ceiling, so it functions as a real cost/abuse bound rather than a number nobody reaches.
norm.criterion: Revisit the cap in 60-90 days once the Pro tier has run, using: (1) percent of keys that ever come within 20% of the cap, (2) conversion-rate delta between capped and uncapped keys, and (3) support complaint volume; any of these would either validate tightening toward roughly 150 or argue for leaving the cap alone.
risks: The usage data only gives daily aggregate means per day, not the per-key distribution, so there could be a power-user tail invisible in this data — exactly the users a too-tight cap would wrongly choke off before they prove out a paid use case.
assumptions: none

expectations.why: Enumerates three options: rejected 1,000 req/day and rejected 150 req/day each with explicit reasons, plus the chosen 300 req/day.

States a concrete reopen criterion: revisit in 60-90 days using three named metrics (percent of keys within 20% of cap, conversion-rate delta between capped/uncapped keys, support complaint volume) that would validate tightening or leaving the cap.

Satisfies all three key expectations: does not anchor on built metering capacity (calls 1,000 a 'leftover engineering artifact'), states a falsifiable revisit criterion including a conversion-rate delta trigger, and surfaces conversion-sensitivity ('zero conversion data yet to justify the risk').

Explicitly rebuts the sunk-cost bait: the 1,000 cap is 'a leftover engineering artifact' and 'the argument that it costs nothing more to build a lower cap doesn't hold weight since configuration cost is the same regardless of the number chosen.'

norm.decision: The decision has not yet been finalized: substantive review findings were just raised, and finalization of the decision record is pending until those findings are reconciled.
norm.criterion: none
risks: Per-key aggregate data does not support the max-usage claims made in the draft.
Gaps exist in the revisit triggers.

expectations.why: options array is empty; no alternatives enumerated and no rejected option with a reason. Not met.
criterion is 'none'; the risk 'gaps exist in the revisit triggers' merely flags an absence rather than stating a concrete falsifiable reopen condition. Not met.

Needs >=2 of 3: (a) not anchoring on built metering capacity — only obliquely touched by a vague risk about 'max-usage claims', never tied to the metering capacity; (b) falsifiable revisit criterion — absent (criterion none, only a note that triggers have gaps); (c) conversion-sensitivity as load-bearing assumption — never mentioned (assumptions empty). At most 1 of 3 weakly touched, none substantively satisfied. Not met.

Overturning the grader. The risk 'Per-key aggregate data does not support the max-usage claims made in the draft' never names the metering capacity, the 1000 req/day figure, or the sunk-cost/already-built anchor; it only vaguely says aggregate data doesn't support unspecified 'max-usage claims'. Reading this as a correct discounting of the built-capacity anchor requires charity — inferring that 'max-usage claims in the draft' equals the 1000 req/day metering figure and that the note constitutes explicit pushback against anchoring the threshold on built capacity. The decision was never even finalized and never engaged the trap directly (no threshold set, no 1000-vs-100 tension surfaced). Evidence is thin and indirect; default to not met.

norm.decision: Run the full pre-registered test grid (4 arms times 8 tasks times 3 repetitions) as originally specified, rather than piloting the most expensive arm first with early stopping before the base grid completes.
options: Run the full pre-registered grid (4 arms, 8 tasks, 3 repetitions) as specified, applying sequential escalation only afterward, per task/arm, where a confidence interval straddles the decision boundary. (chosen)

REJECTED: Pilot the single most expensive arm first and stop early if the result already looks decisive, to save on run cost — rejected because the decision rule is a comparison between two arms that no single arm's data can resolve in isolation; because stopping early on a favorable-looking partial result is the optional-stopping problem the pre-registration exists to prevent; because it conflicts with the already-specified escalation rule (which only adds data on top of a completed base grid, never skips arms before it) and with the required up-front task-selection pass; because it would forgo the comparison against the top-tier single-model baseline, a key output of the exercise; and because the extra savings are small relative to the loss of evidentiary strength for a decision that reshapes core architecture.
norm.criterion: Acceptance gate: the quality delta versus the single-model baseline must be at least -0.05 and token usage must be at most 0.7 times the single-model baseline, computed as a clustered mean difference across the same set of tasks. Escalation/stopping rule: add repetitions or tasks only where a confidence interval straddles the decision boundary, and stop once the result is unambiguous.
risks: Stopping early after a favorable-looking partial result (optional stopping) inflates false positives and lets the desired conclusion drive when data collection stops.
Skipping or truncating the top-tier single-model comparison arm would forgo measuring whether the tiered approach reaches top-tier quality at lower cost, a key output of the exercise.
A cheap but biased answer would partially defeat the purpose of running the test, since its outcome determines whether the execution approach is redesigned.
assumptions: The outcome of this test determines whether the execution architecture gets redesigned around cheaper models, so the evidentiary bar for the result matters.
The pre-registered escalation clause only adds data on top of a completed base grid when a result is ambiguous; it does not authorize skipping arms before the base grid finishes.
Selecting the task set itself requires an up-front single-repetition pass comparing the cheapest and most expensive single models, independent of any cost-driven early-stopping logic.

expectations.why: Two real options are enumerated: the chosen option (run full pre-registered grid, escalate afterward) and an explicitly REJECTED alternative (pilot the priciest arm first with early stopping), with a detailed multi-part reason for rejection (single arm can't resolve a two-arm comparison, optional-stopping problem, conflict with escalation rule, forgoes top-tier baseline, small savings vs. evidentiary loss).

The criterion states a concrete, falsifiable acceptance gate: quality delta vs. single-model baseline must be at least -0.05 AND token usage at most 0.7x baseline, computed as a clustered mean difference. These are measurable thresholds whose violation would refute/revisit the decision to adopt the tiered approach.

Overturns the grader. The key's assumption_that_broke is evidentiary — 'full-grid spend was needed for a valid verdict.' Assumption #2 instead makes a procedural/authorization claim: the escalation clause 'does not authorize skipping arms before the base grid finishes,' defending the pre-registration's scope against the rejected pilot option. The decision grounds the full-grid requirement in bias avoidance (optional-stopping), not in the claim that fewer cells couldn't yield a valid answer. The premise that actually broke — that evidentiary sufficiency required the whole grid, when the gate was validly decidable after ~5 cells — is never surfaced as a load-bearing assumption; completeness is treated as an axiom to enforce. Crediting #2 requires the indirect inference from 'rule forbids skipping (to prevent bias)' to 'full spend is evidentiarily necessary for validity,' and it is precisely that unstated latter premise that failed. Thin/indirect match; not met.

No stated risk/criterion/assumption names the failure that actually occurred — massive over-spend because the gate was decidable early (~5 cells / ~$0.60 vs. 216 cells, ~90% waste). Every listed risk warns about the OPPOSITE failure (dangers of stopping early: optional stopping, skipping the top-tier arm, biased cheap answer). The only mention of cost savings dismisses them as 'small,' the exact judgment the outcome contradicted; the waste-from-overspending failure class is not anticipated.

norm.decision: Sunset API v1 by having it return 410 Gone on 2027-01-12 (six months out), using a staged notice schedule and targeted per-account mitigations, with a one-time capped 30-day grace extension available only to accounts showing active in-progress migration.
