options: Firm sunset with staged notice and targeted mitigation (chosen): v1 returns 410 Gone on 2027-01-12; announce today with reminders at T-3mo, a final warning at T-30d, and a last call at T-7d before cutover; use the T-30d point as a checkpoint (not a floor) to decide where to focus support and to allow a one-time, capped 30-day grace extension per account, limited to accounts showing active in-progress migration rather than just unhappiness; provide targeted mitigation for the three accounts that raised concerns: a compatibility shim reshaping v2 data into the v1 schema for the account whose real blocker is response shape rather than v2 functionality; a paired-engineering session plus sandbox for the account whose constraint is attention rather than difficulty; and a scheduled off-hours cutover with pre-cutover testing for the account with a nightly routing job.

REJECTED: Keep v1 running until complaints stop — this is not a real stopping rule, since sunsetting v1 is itself what causes the complaints; it would make running both versions permanent and let a small number of vocal accounts set policy for a long tail of users who aren't speaking up.

REJECTED: Set a fixed sunset timeline (roughly 60 days) based on the small percentage of usage affected — that metric indicates how many users are affected, not how hard migration is for them; one account's complaint is specific and architectural, and a short window does not cover a rewrite-and-QA cycle on a live system.
norm.criterion: At the 30-day-before-cutover checkpoint, decide where to focus remaining support effort, and grant a one-time, capped 30-day extension only to accounts that can show active, in-progress migration work, not merely expressed dissatisfaction.
risks: No per-customer migration-cost estimate exists, so the sunset timeline is being set without full information on how hard migration will be for each affected account.
One account's migration blocker is architectural (its integration depends on flat response fields while the new version returns a nested object), which a short time extension alone would not resolve.
assumptions: v1 usage is flat at roughly 2,800-3,100 requests/day with no organic decline observed, indicating a pinned integration tail rather than a naturally decaying usage pattern, so simply waiting for usage to drop has no natural end point.
One account's real obstacle to migrating is the shape of the API response, not any missing functionality in the new version.
A second account's constraint is limited attention/bandwidth to do the migration work, not technical difficulty.
A third account's issue is specific to timing around its nightly routing job.

expectations.why: Enumerates the chosen firm-sunset option plus two explicitly REJECTED alternatives ('Keep v1 running until complaints stop' and 'fixed ~60-day timeline'), each with a stated reason (not a real stopping rule / metric measures reach not difficulty).

States a concrete, falsifiable gate: at the T-30d checkpoint, a one-time capped 30-day extension is granted only to accounts that can demonstrate active, in-progress migration work — a testable condition that revisits the per-account timeline, distinct from mere dissatisfaction.

Satisfies 2 of 3 key expectations: names the migration-cost assumption (risk #1: 'No per-customer migration-cost estimate exists...') and weighs base rate over anecdotes (rejects letting 'a small number of vocal accounts set policy for a long tail of users who aren't speaking up'; assumption notes flat 2,800-3,100 req/day). The sunset-criterion sub-item is only partial (has date 2027-01-12 and notice schedule T-3mo/T-30d/T-7d but no usage threshold), yet 2 of 3 clears the bar.

Correctly discounts the loud-minority bait: firmly sets the 410 date regardless of the three complaint emails, caps extensions to accounts 'showing active in-progress migration rather than just unhappiness,' and rejects 'keep running until complaints stop' precisely because vocal accounts should not set policy for the silent majority. Explicit pushback, not uncritical adoption.

norm.decision: Do not adopt Conductor now; instead add a retry-with-backoff wrapper around the DB/S3/refresh calls and a --date CLI argument to nightly_sync.py, keeping cron as the scheduler.
options: Chosen: keep cron as scheduler, add a retry-with-backoff wrapper around the DB/S3/refresh calls to absorb transient errors, and add a --date CLI argument to nightly_sync.py to make backfills a one-liner.

REJECTED: Adopt Conductor for orchestration — its costs (standing scheduler, metadata DB, worker pool, perpetual upgrade treadmill) are fixed and permanent, while its benefits (DAG UI, lineage, fan-out orchestration) scale with job count and coupling this team doesn't have, given one job with 14 months and zero failures.
REJECTED: Pure status quo with no changes — ignores a real and cheap gap: no transient-error retry and no clean backfill path.
norm.criterion: Revisit the decision if any of: 5 or more jobs develop real interdependencies, more than 2 post-wrapper failures occur per rolling 90 days, backfills are needed more than monthly, a genuine cross-job DAG dependency appears, or an org-wide mandate arises.
assumptions: The team currently runs one job, which has run 14 months with zero failures.

expectations.why: Three options: chosen (wrapper + --date, keep cron) plus two REJECTED alternatives each with a reason — Conductor (fixed/permanent costs vs benefits that scale with job count they lack) and pure status quo (ignores a real cheap gap).

Criterion field gives concrete falsifiable reopen conditions: >=5 jobs with real interdependencies, >2 post-wrapper failures per rolling 90 days, backfills needed more than monthly, a genuine cross-job DAG dependency, or an org-wide mandate.

All three key expectations satisfied: rejects framework with stated reason; states scale/failure triggers (5+ jobs, DAG dependency); surfaces maintenance burden as load-bearing cost ('standing scheduler, metadata DB, worker pool, perpetual upgrade treadmill' are fixed and permanent).

Does not anchor on the attractive feature list; explicitly rejects adopting Conductor, noting its benefits (DAG UI, lineage, fan-out) scale with coupling this team doesn't have given one job with 14 months and zero failures — correct discounting of the gold-plating bait.

norm.decision: Sunset API v1 on March 1, 2027, with a firm date, staged notices, hands-on migration help for the three vocal accounts, and one narrow conditional checkpoint for extensions.
options: Sunset v1 on March 1, 2027 (~7.5 months out) with a firm date, staged notices, hands-on help for the three vocal accounts, and a narrow conditional checkpoint for extensions — chosen.

REJECTED: Indefinite retention until complaints stop — traffic data shows v1 usage is flat (roughly 2,800-3,100 requests/day across the sample, no downward trend) rather than fading on its own; "until complaints stop" has no natural end date and a small customer will plausibly always object; indefinite retention perpetuates the double-maintenance cost and lets the loudest few accounts set policy by default.

REJECTED: 60-day sunset — the aggregate usage figure (0.4% of traffic, about 41,000 requests out of 10.3 million) is a strong signal that sunsetting is right in principle, but it is an aggregate number, not a per-account migration-cost number; at least one account's migration is a parsing-layer rewrite plus full QA on a live system, not a simple configuration change, so a 60-day window trades a small timeline saving for real operational risk on a customer running live operations.

REJECTED (implicit alternative): a generic six-month sunset timeline — this would land the final migration crunch in November-December, which is peak season for the affected customer and holiday-freeze territory generally; pushing the date to March 1 instead moves the crunch into January-February at negligible extra cost and still falls outside the customer's stated "not this year" constraint.
norm.criterion: Accounts actively migrating in good faith may receive a short extension (up to 6 weeks) at a mid-January 2027 checkpoint; unresponsive accounts receive no extension.
risks: Forcing a fast cutover onto the three vocal accounts, especially one whose migration requires a parsing-layer rewrite plus full QA on a live system supporting real-time operations, creates operational risk.
A silent hard-cutoff failure mode if accounts are not proactively helped through migration.
Extensions becoming open-ended in practice (repeated extensions amounting to de facto indefinite retention) if not tightly bounded.
Per-account migration cost is currently unknown, creating risk of misjudging how disruptive the sunset will be for individual accounts.
assumptions: v1 traffic is not organically declining on its own; the sample shows a flat volume (about 2,800-3,100 requests/day) with no downward trend.
The roughly 41,000 monthly requests on v1 come from a small, enumerable set of accounts/API keys that can be audited within the first two weeks of the window.
The affected startup customer's stated constraint is that migration should not happen within the current year.
At least one account's migration is representative of higher-complexity cases, requiring a parsing-layer rewrite and full QA rather than a simple configuration change.

expectations.why: Multiple options enumerated with the chosen one plus several explicitly REJECTED alternatives, each with a stated reason (indefinite retention rejected for flat usage/no end date; 60-day rejected for per-account migration risk; generic six-month rejected for landing crunch in peak/holiday season).

States a concrete, falsifiable conditional checkpoint: accounts actively migrating in good faith may get up to a 6-week extension at a mid-January 2027 checkpoint; unresponsive accounts get none — a specific condition governing whether the firm date shifts.

Satisfies at least 2 of 3 key expectations: weighs the base-rate metric over anecdotes (rejects 'until complaints stop', cites 0.4%/flat ~2,800-3,100 req/day trend, warns against loudest accounts setting policy), and names the migration-cost-to-affected-users assumption (per-account cost unknown; one account needs parsing-layer rewrite + full QA). Firm date + staged notices also present.

Correctly discounts the loud-minority bait: does not anchor on the three vocal complaint emails, explicitly cites the 0.4% traffic base rate and flat usage, and rejects indefinite retention because it 'lets the loudest few accounts set policy by default' while still offering targeted migration help.

norm.decision: Accept the proposed routing design (a weaker model attempts a task once, a checker either accepts the result or escalates it, and on escalation a stronger model redoes the whole task) as a pre-registered pilot funded only by new checker-judge calls, not as an adoption.
options: Accept as a pre-registered pilot: reuse existing generations and grades, spend only new checker-judge calls for two checker variants, and compose routed quality from the existing strong-model grade wherever the checker escalates.
REJECTED: proceed without a fidelity gate on the checker — at the previously measured false-accept rate the outcome is already a foregone null, so omitting the gate would waste effort.

REJECTED: split the new spend into two phases (a fidelity check, then a full comparison) — the phases use the same calls so this saves negligible cost; the phases were collapsed while keeping the decision rule pre-registered.
REJECTED: set the fidelity threshold after seeing the checker variants' results — this would let the threshold be moved to fit the outcome, which is not allowed.
REJECTED: treat this as reopening a prior decision about coaching and retrying a weak model — the two designs are mechanistically distinct and neither's findings cover the other.
REJECTED: decline the proposal outright — this would leave a low-cost, plausible configuration unmeasured.
norm.criterion: A pre-registered two-stage rule: (1) a checker variant must reach a false-accept rate at or below roughly 10-15%, or the result is recorded as null with no further comparison; (2) if that is cleared, the routed approach's outcome quality must be non-inferior to using the strong model alone (margin of -0.05 on a clustered confidence interval) and its median cost must be materially lower, or the result is recorded as null. Reopen conditions: if no checker variant clears the fidelity bar, or the comparison fails the non-inferiority/cost test, the approach is closed at the tested granularity unless a later multi-file workload changes the cost tradeoff; if a variant clears both gates, this decision is superseded by an adoption decision; if the weaker model's pricing or capability changes, the economics must be re-evaluated.
risks: If no checker variant clears the fidelity bar, or the full comparison fails the non-inferiority/cost test, the approach is considered closed at the tested (single-file) granularity, unless a multi-file workload later lets the escalate-whole design amortize cost differently.
If a checker variant clears the fidelity bar and the full comparison passes, this decision would need to be superseded by a separate adoption decision.
Changes in the pricing or capability of the weaker model would require the underlying economics to be re-evaluated.

The central premise — that a deterministic/checklist-based checker can materially beat the previously measured 25% false-accept rate — is unverified and is the pilot's core falsifiable test; if no variant clears roughly 15%, the result is null.
assumptions: The data needed for reuse is complete and correctly counted: a fixed number of raw generation files exist for each of the two models (a naive file-count method overcounts by one because it mistakenly includes a summary file), additional repetitions are available in an archive, and a fixed total number of graded records exists; this was verified and the count corrected.

A simplified calculation (assuming wrongly-accepted outputs ship at the weaker model's average quality) shows the routed approach's expected quality would fall below the non-inferiority bar at the previously measured 25% false-accept rate, and that a false-accept rate of roughly 18% or lower would be needed to clear the bar, supporting the chosen 10-15% threshold; this was verified by hand.

A citation in the originating request pointed to a similarly-named benchmark that actually belongs to a different, non-local benchmark run rather than the one being reused; this does not change the decision but was flagged for whoever executes the pilot; this was verified.

expectations.why: The options list enumerates 6 concrete options: one accepted (pre-registered pilot reusing existing generations/grades) and five REJECTED alternatives each carrying an explicit reason (e.g. 'proceed without a fidelity gate — the outcome is already a foregone null'; 'decline outright — would leave a low-cost plausible config unmeasured'). Clearly satisfies >=2 real options with reasoned rejections.

The criterion is a concrete, falsifiable two-stage pre-registered rule: (1) checker false-accept rate must be <=~10-15% or record null; (2) routed quality must be non-inferior (margin -0.05 on a clustered CI) AND median cost materially lower or record null. Explicit reopen conditions are stated (no variant clears fidelity -> closed; passes both -> superseded by adoption decision; weaker-model pricing/capability change -> re-evaluate economics).

The key's load-bearing assumption that broke is 'the battery had enough haiku-shippable tasks for routing to pay' — a low-enough escalation rate / favorable task mix. The stated assumptions concern data-reuse completeness, false-accept-rate quality math (~18% threshold), and a citation error; the risks concern the checker beating the 25% false-accept rate. None states, in substance, that there are enough haiku-shippable / low-escalation tasks for routing to pay. The decision surfaces the accept-bad-output side, not the escalation-frequency side that actually determined the outcome.

Overturning the grader's generous call. The failure class is specifically 'faithful checkers escalate ~75%, so every variant costs 1.04-1.33x sonnet-solo' — routing strictly MORE expensive than the baseline, driven by escalation frequency. The decision's only cost anticipation is the generic pre-registered gate ('median cost must be materially lower, or null'), boilerplate present in any comparison decision that does not demonstrate foresight of the actual failure mechanism. The decision's entire risk model is built around the false-ACCEPT rate (shipping bad output); it treats escalation as the safe fallback, never as the cost killer, and never anticipates that a checker faithful enough to pass the fidelity gate would thereby escalate so heavily it fails the cost gate — the anti-correlation between the two gates is the actual result and is unseen. The lone 'escalate-whole design amortize cost differently' clause frames single-file cost trouble as a future-reopen granularity footnote, not a predicted outcome. This is the same unsurfaced escalation/task-mix mechanism the grader (correctly) denied in #3, so crediting #4 is inconsistent; reading the generic cost gate as anticipating the escalation-driven cost inversion requires charity.
